CHANGELOG.md and validation impact assessment required for every production deployment.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: TOTP clock skew causing signature verification failures during inspection. Mitigation: 30-second window tolerance with server-side clock synchronization via NTP; clock drift monitoring alert. Risk: S3 Object Lock Compliance mode prevents deletion even by AWS support; ensure correct 15-year retention period before enabling as it is irreversible. Mitigation: staging environment validation before production enablement. Tradeoff: custom JWT service over Auth0/Cognito maintains 21 CFR Part 11 unique identity binding control but increases maintenance burden; document auth module as a SOC 2 scoped system. Tradeoff: Aurora Multi-AZ over serverless Aurora provides predictable latency required for 3s p99 SLA at the cost of always-on instance cost.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected custom JWT with TOTP over Cognito for explicit 21 CFR Part 11 binding control. Chose S3 Object Lock Compliance mode over Governance mode; Compliance prevents even root account deletion satisfying Part 11 immutability requirement. SHA-256 hash chaining selected for audit trail over blockchain; simpler to implement, audit, and explain to FDA inspectors. Aurora Multi-AZ over RDS PostgreSQL for automatic failover without manual intervention. eCTD schema validation artifacts embedded as classpath resources rather than fetched at runtime to satisfy air-gap and reproducibility requirements.

metadata.notes: FDA 21 CFR Part 11 clinical trial data API with electronic signatures, immutable audit trail, and HIPAA compliance.

profile.domain: Drug Discovery High-Throughput Screening Data Management

project_goal.value: High-throughput screening data management platform for drug discovery labs enabling scientists to ingest, query, and visualize results from automated plate-reader assays across millions of compound-target interaction data points with sub-second query response times
project_goal.rationale: Existing Excel-based workflows cannot scale to modern HTS throughput of 100K+ compounds per campaign

target_users.value: Medicinal chemists, assay scientists, and computational biologists conducting drug discovery screening campaigns
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified in discovery workshops with the HTS group

project_class.rationale: React dashboard for scientist users plus FastAPI backend for plate reader integration and query API

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, analytics, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: UI for scientists, API for plate reader ingest, OLAP queries, and overnight batch normalization jobs

complexity_level.rationale: Millions of activity measurements requiring sub-second OLAP queries demands column-store database architecture

risk_level.rationale: Confidential compound activity data is valuable IP; internal-only exposure reduces external attack surface

data_sensitivity.rationale: Compound-target activity profiles are proprietary drug discovery IP; potential export control applicability

external_exposure.rationale: System accessible only within corporate VPN; no public or partner-facing access

access_model.value: Okta SSO with RBAC; roles: scientist (read/write own assays), lead (all assays in project), admin (system config). MFA enforced via Okta policy.
access_model.rationale: Corporate Okta deployment standardizes SSO across internal tools

feature_scope.value: Plate reader data ingest via API, Z-prime QC scoring, hit threshold flagging, dose-response curve fitting, compound library browser, multi-assay cross-screen comparison, batch normalization jobs, CSV/SDF export
feature_scope.rationale: Full HTS data lifecycle from raw plate reader output to prioritized hit list

mvp_scope.value: Plate data ingest, Z-prime QC, activity hit flagging above configurable threshold, compound library browser, and CSV export
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum feature set to replace Excel for first screening campaign

security_baseline.value: Okta SSO with MFA, TLS 1.3 in transit, encryption at rest on all EBS and S3, VPN-only access, API rate limiting, audit log of all data exports
security_baseline.rationale: Protect confidential compound IP from unauthorized access or bulk exfiltration

frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise and existing React component library

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI async performance for plate reader ingest; Python for scientific library access

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for activity measurements OLAP; PostgreSQL 16 for compound metadata and user data; Redis 7 for query result caching
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse column-store provides sub-second aggregation over billions of rows; PostgreSQL for relational metadata

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1, internal VPC only
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers within corporate AWS account behind internal ALB; no public IP

privacy_retention_policy.value: Compound activity data retained indefinitely as proprietary research asset; export logs retained 3 years; user activity logs 1 year
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Drug discovery data has long-term IP value; export logs for DLP compliance

future_scope.value: Automated dose-response curve fitting with Hill equation, ML-based hit prioritization, integration with compound procurement system
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap after initial HTS workflow validation

constraints.value: Sub-second p95 query latency on 500M row activity table; no third-party SaaS for compound data (IP protection); budget under $4000/month
constraints.rationale: Scientist productivity requires fast queries; IP policy prevents compound data leaving corporate AWS

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards on Prometheus metrics; structured JSON logging; Slack alerts for batch job failures and ingest errors
observability_baseline.rationale: Team uses Grafana/Prometheus stack for all internal services

execution_preference.value: Agile 2-week sprints; trunk-based development with feature flags for progressive rollout to scientist groups
execution_preference.rationale: Iterative delivery enables early scientist feedback before full campaign rollout

compliance_context.value: Internal IP protection policies; potential EAR export control if compound data involves dual-use chemical structures; DLP controls required for compound data export
compliance_context.rationale: Compound-target activity profiles may fall under export control regulations depending on compound class

project.goal: HTS data management platform with sub-second OLAP queries over millions of assay measurements
project.target_users: Medicinal chemists, assay scientists, computational biologists

frontend.state: React Query for server state; Zustand for UI state
frontend.charts: Recharts for dose-response and scatter plots

backend.framework: FastAPI with async endpoints; Celery for batch jobs

security.auth: Okta OIDC with PKCE

reasoner_reviews.product: The Z-prime quality scoring and configurable hit threshold are the most critical analytical features; assay scientists cannot proceed without automated QC. The dose-response curve visualization in Phase 2 is highly valued but correctly deferred. The compound library browser needs fast full-text search over compound names and structures.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: ClickHouse is the right choice for activity_measurements given the query pattern: aggregations over compound_id and target_protein_id across hundreds of millions of rows. The MergeTree engine with compound_id as the ORDER BY key collocates all activity rows for a compound on the same shard, enabling fast per-compound aggregations. PostgreSQL handles compound metadata, user data, and application tables. The dual-database pattern requires careful data consistency management at the application layer.
reasoner_reviews.security: VPN-only exposure significantly reduces the attack surface. The key remaining risk is bulk compound data exfiltration by insiders; audit logging of all CSV/SDF exports with user identity and record count is the primary control. Okta MFA is correctly enforced at the corporate level. Redis query result cache must not persist confidential compound data longer than the session TTL.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Sub-second p95 on 500M rows is achievable with ClickHouse but requires careful schema design: materialized views for pre-aggregated hit counts per assay, and Redis caching for repeated scientist queries against the same dataset. Batch normalization jobs should run off-peak to avoid competing with scientist query workload.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The round 1 plan did not address ClickHouse replication topology or backup strategy. A single ClickHouse node is a single point of failure for the entire analytical query capability. This must be resolved before production launch.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with domain modules: ingest (PlateReaderIngestionAPI), analysis (ZPrimeCalculator, HitThresholdService), queries (ActivityQueryService using ClickHouse async driver), batch (Celery tasks for NormalizationJob and DoseResponseFitter), and export (DataExportService with audit logging).
backend.api_patterns: REST API at /api/v1. POST /ingest/plates (batch plate reader push), GET /assays/{id}/qc (Z-prime and plate quality), GET /compounds/{id}/activity (all activity for compound across assays), GET /screens/{id}/hits (hit list above threshold), POST /exports (initiate CSV/SDF export with audit), GET /exports/{id}/download (signed S3 URL after job completion).
backend.background_jobs: Celery workers on separate ECS tasks: NormalizationJob (per-plate negative/positive control normalization, percent inhibition calculation), ZPrimeCalculator (per-plate Z-prime and signal-to-background), HitExporter (S3 CSV/SDF generation for large result sets). Beat scheduler triggers overnight normalization sweep for any unnormalized plates.
backend.scaling_notes: FastAPI API tasks autoscale on CPU 60% target. Celery workers autoscale on queue depth. ClickHouse query concurrency limited to 20 simultaneous queries; Redis caching of top-100 most repeated queries reduces ClickHouse load by estimated 70% for repeated scientist sessions.
backend.backend_risks: ClickHouse INSERT performance degrades with too-frequent small inserts; batch plate inserts into a staging table and merge to ClickHouse in 1000-row batches via background worker.

frontend.app_structure: React 18 SPA with route-based code splitting. Pages: ScreenDashboard, AssayDetail (with QC metrics and plate heatmaps), CompoundBrowser (searchable compound library), HitList (filterable by assay and threshold), ExportManager.
frontend.pages_and_flows: ScreenDashboard shows active campaigns with plate count, hit rate, and Z-prime trend. AssayDetail renders 96-well plate heatmaps colored by percent inhibition with Z-prime score badge. CompoundBrowser provides full-text search over compound_name and CAS number with structure image thumbnails. HitList allows multi-column sorting and CSV export trigger with download progress.
frontend.state_management: React Query for all API data fetching with 5-minute stale time for activity data. Zustand store for selected assay/compound filters and UI preference state.
frontend.performance_notes: Plate heatmap renders 384 well cells via canvas rather than SVG to avoid DOM node limits. Virtualized list for compound browser results above 1000 items. Recharts dose-response curve lazy-loaded per compound on demand.
frontend.frontend_risks: Large compound library CSV exports can exceed browser download buffer limits; use streaming download via signed S3 URL rather than proxying through the API.

security.auth_design: Okta OIDC with PKCE for SPA. FastAPI backend validates Okta-issued JWT on every request. 1-hour access token with silent refresh via Okta SDK.
security.authorization_model: RBAC: scientist role can read/write assays within their assigned project. lead role can access all assays in their project and approve exports. admin role manages projects, compound libraries, and system config. FastAPI Depends() decorators enforce role checks per endpoint.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All data exports logged to export_audit table with user_id, export_type, filter_criteria, record_count, ip_address, and timestamp. Redis cache keys include user_id to prevent cross-user cache leakage. ClickHouse query logs retained 1 year.
security.security_risks: Redis cache must be flushed on user role downgrade to prevent reading cached results from previously higher-privileged queries.

data.entities: ClickHouse: activity_measurements (compound_id UInt64, target_protein_id UInt32, assay_id UInt32, plate_id UInt32, well_position String, raw_signal Float64, percent_inhibition Float64, is_hit UInt8, z_prime Float32, measured_at DateTime) ENGINE=MergeTree ORDER BY (compound_id, assay_id, measured_at). PostgreSQL: compounds (id BIGINT PK, compound_name TEXT, cas_number TEXT, smiles TEXT, mol_weight REAL, library_id FK), screening_plates (id UUID PK, assay_id FK, plate_barcode TEXT, instrument_id FK, run_at TIMESTAMPTZ, z_prime REAL, signal_to_background REAL, status TEXT), assay_protocols (id UUID, assay_name, target_protein_id FK, positive_control TEXT, negative_control TEXT, hit_threshold_pct REAL), target_proteins (id UUID, gene_symbol, uniprot_id, therapeutic_area).
data.storage_design: ClickHouse cluster with 2-shard 2-replica topology using ReplicatedMergeTree. PostgreSQL Aurora Multi-AZ for metadata. S3 for exported CSV/SDF files with 90-day lifecycle to Glacier.
data.analytics_events: ClickHouse materialized view hit_counts_by_assay pre-aggregates compound hit counts per assay for dashboard loading.
data.data_risks: ClickHouse ReplicatedMergeTree requires ZooKeeper or ClickHouse Keeper for replica coordination; plan ClickHouse Keeper co-deployment in same ECS cluster.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate: API service (2-10 tasks), Celery worker tasks (1-5), ClickHouse nodes (2-shard 2-replica on EC2 r6i.2xlarge), PostgreSQL Aurora Multi-AZ, Redis ElastiCache, internal ALB. All within private VPC subnets; no public IP on any service.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff lint, mypy, pytest with testcontainers ClickHouse and PostgreSQL, 85% coverage, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy. Feature flags via LaunchDarkly for progressive rollout to scientist groups.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus metrics from FastAPI via prometheus-fastapi-instrumentator. Grafana dashboards: ingest throughput (plates/hour), ClickHouse query latency histogram (p50/p95/p99), batch job completion rates, hit rate trends per campaign. Slack webhook alerts for batch job failure and ingest error rate >5%.
devops.devops_risks: ClickHouse on EC2 requires manual patching and monitoring not provided by a managed service; allocate dedicated DevOps capacity for ClickHouse cluster operations.

ISS-001.title: ClickHouse single-node SPOF not addressed
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan specified a single ClickHouse node with no replication strategy, creating a complete analytical query outage risk on node failure.

history.note: Architect did not specify ClickHouse topology

ISS-002.title: Redis cache may leak confidential compound data across user sessions
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan used global cache keys without user-scoping, risking scientist A reading cached compound activity results from scientist B's query.

ISS-003.title: No export audit trail for DLP compliance
ISS-003.detail: The compliance context requires DLP controls on compound data export but round 1 plan had no export audit logging mechanism.

history.note: Export API had no audit logging

focus_issues.title: Redis cache may leak confidential compound data across user sessions
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 plan used global cache keys without user-scoping, risking cross-user compound activity data leakage.

focus_issues.title: No export audit trail for DLP compliance
focus_issues.detail: Compliance context requires DLP controls on compound export but no audit logging was specified in round 1.

latest_recommendations: Add user_id to all Redis cache keys to prevent cross-session compound data leakage
Implement export_audit table with user_id, filter_criteria, and record_count for DLP compliance
Document ClickHouse replication topology with ZooKeeper/ClickHouse Keeper coordination
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: HTS Screening Data Platform Architecture v1

previous_audits.summary: Good architectural choices with ClickHouse and FastAPI for HTS scale, but critical security and compliance gaps around cache isolation and export auditing must be resolved before production.
strengths: ClickHouse MergeTree schema design is appropriate for compound activity query patterns
Celery batch job design for overnight normalization is well-structured
React canvas-based plate heatmap approach is correct for 384-well rendering performance
concerns: Redis cache keys are not user-scoped, enabling cross-scientist compound data leakage
No export audit logging despite compliance_context requiring DLP controls
ClickHouse single-node topology is a single point of failure for analytical queries
blocking_issues: Redis cache cross-user leakage is a high-severity security issue given confidential data sensitivity
Missing export audit trail violates stated compliance_context DLP requirement
recommendations: Scope all Redis cache keys with user_id prefix or hash to prevent cross-session data access
Add export_audit table recording user_id, export_type, filter_criteria, and record_count for every CSV/SDF export
Specify ClickHouse ReplicatedMergeTree with 2-shard 2-replica topology and ClickHouse Keeper coordination

issue_updates.detail: Round 2 plan specifies 2-shard 2-replica ReplicatedMergeTree with ClickHouse Keeper

issue_updates.title: Redis cache cross-user leakage
issue_updates.detail: Round 1 plan had no cache key scoping design
