target_output.workflows: Evidence intake workflow: custodian logs in, navigates to Evidence Intake, enters case number and evidence description, application generates Code 128 barcode and writes evidence_items record, custodian prints barcode label from connected label printer. Chain-of-custody transfer: analyst scans evidence barcode at item scan field, scans own badge barcode at analyst scan field, confirms transfer in dialog, Electron main process writes custody_transfer_event and updates evidence_items.current_custodian_id in a single SQLite transaction. Report signing workflow: analyst submits completed examination_record; supervisor opens report review screen, reviews findings and attached photos, clicks Approve - ReportModule calls pdf-lib to render PDF/A-3, node-forge retrieves supervisor private key from CNG, signs PDF hash with RSA-2048 SHA-256, embeds signature and certificate chain, writes signed PDF to shared attachments directory, sets reports.signed_at and signature_hash.
target_output.data_model: evidence_items (id, case_id, barcode VARCHAR, description, received_at, current_location_id, current_custodian_id, status ENUM intaken/in_analysis/in_storage/disposed, created_at). custody_transfer_events (id, evidence_item_id, from_analyst_id, to_analyst_id, scanned_barcode, transfer_timestamp, notes). examination_records (id, evidence_item_id, analyst_id, examination_type, findings_text, photo_attachment_paths JSON relative paths from shared dir, started_at, completed_at, status ENUM draft/submitted/approved/rejected). reports (id, case_id, examination_record_ids JSON, draft_pdf_path, signed_pdf_path relative to shared dir, supervisor_id, signed_at, signature_hash SHA-256). audit_log (id, user_id, action, table_name, record_id, timestamp - append-only via AFTER INSERT triggers, no DELETE privilege on app role). Attachment base path: C:/ForensicLIMS/attachments/{case_id}/{evidence_id}/.
target_output.api_design: Electron contextBridge IPC channels. evidence:intake({caseId, description, custodianId}) -> {evidenceId, barcode}. evidence:transfer({evidenceId, scannedBarcode, toAnalystId}) -> CustodyTransferEvent. examination:create({evidenceId, type, findings}) -> ExaminationRecord. examination:attach-photo({examinationId, filePath}) -> relativePath. report:generate({examinationId}) -> {draftPdfPath}. report:sign({reportId, supervisorId}) -> {signedPdfPath, signatureHash}. admin:list-audit-log({startDate, endDate}) -> AuditLogEntry[]. admin:export-audit-csv({startDate, endDate}) -> csvFilePath. All handlers validate JWT session and RBAC role before execution.
target_output.security_and_compliance: SQLCipher AES-256 encryption for SQLite database passphrase derived from application master key stored in Windows DPAPI. Append-only audit_log enforced by SQLite AFTER INSERT triggers on all entity tables; application role has no DELETE privilege on audit_log. Local RBAC enforced in every IPC handler before database access. PDF signing: pdf-lib renders PDF/A-3, node-forge signs SHA-256 hash of PDF bytes using analyst RSA-2048 private key retrieved from Windows CNG (CNG API via CNG bridge); key is non-exportable, installed by lab admin from PKCS#12 bundle generated by lab CA. Certificate chain embedded in signed PDF for offline verification. JWT sessions 4-hour expiry stored in Electron safeStorage. ISO 17025 and ASCLD compliance: all evidence transfers require barcode scan confirmation; audit log is primary compliance artifact.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: electron-builder generates MSI installer including Electron runtime, Node.js 20, SQLite with SQLCipher binaries, all npm dependencies. Lab IT deploys MSI to isolated workstations via group policy or manual install. First-run setup wizard initializes SQLite database, sets SQLCipher passphrase, creates first admin account. Lab admin installs analyst PKCS#12 certificates into Windows CNG from lab CA-generated bundles. GitHub Actions CI on PR: tsc, eslint, vitest unit tests for IPC handlers. Release workflow builds and code-signs MSI with lab IT code-signing certificate. Database migrations via numbered SQL files in /migrations executed on app launch against current schema version.
target_output.observability: Local audit_log in SQLite is the primary compliance record. Application errors logged to rolling log file at C:/ForensicLIMS/logs/ with 30-day retention. Admin panel provides audit log viewer filtered by date, user, and action type, with CSV export. No external monitoring; facility is offline. Lab IT reviews logs during periodic security reviews.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero cloud infrastructure cost; fully on-premises. electron-builder and GitHub Actions free tier for open-source lab tools. SQLite scales to millions of evidence records per workstation before performance concerns. Multi-workstation replication for shared evidence rooms is future scope requiring SQLite replication or migration to PostgreSQL with a local server.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Accreditation MVP (8 weeks): evidence intake with barcode generation, custody transfer with barcode scan, examination record with photo attachment, PDF report with RSA-2048 CNG signing, supervisor approval workflow, admin panel with user management and audit log export. Phase 2 (6 weeks): LIMS instrument result import, case closure workflow with retention date setting, multi-workstation SQLite replication pilot. Phase 3 (roadmap): Mobile companion app for barcode scanning, LIMS full bidirectional integration.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode. All IPC handler functions typed with Zod schema validation on input payloads before database operations. vitest unit tests for all IPC handlers with SQLite in-memory test database. Integration tests for PDF signing flow using test RSA key pair. No network calls anywhere in main or renderer process. Audit log writes are mandatory: any IPC handler modifying entity tables must trigger audit_log write via AuditModule. Code-signing certificate for MSI managed by lab IT, not stored in repository.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: CNG bridge library compatibility with Electron node version. Mitigation: pin electron-rebuild to exact Node.js version bundled with Electron 30 and test CNG operations in CI. Risk: SQLCipher passphrase management - if passphrase lost, database is unrecoverable. Mitigation: passphrase stored in Windows DPAPI by lab admin account; documented recovery procedure in lab IT runbook. Tradeoff: single-workstation SQLite over a shared server database sacrifices multi-analyst concurrent access for offline simplicity in Phase 1.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Signing library: pdf-lib with node-forge via Windows CNG bridge; RSA-2048 SHA-256 with PDF/A-3 embedding. ISS-001 resolved. Attachment storage: shared C:/ForensicLIMS/attachments/ independent of OS user profile. ISS-003 resolved. Multi-workstation replication: deferred to Phase 2 after single-workstation pilot validates accreditation compliance.

metadata.notes: Round 3 forensic evidence desktop app revision; resolves persistent ISS-001 signing gap and ISS-003 attachment path; weak quality as several sections remain minimal.

profile.domain: Astronomy Telescope Observation Image Processing Pipeline

project_goal.value: Cost-optimized data pipeline for an astronomy research center to ingest, calibrate, and archive nightly telescope observation images, replacing manual processing scripts with automated workflows using spot instances to reduce cloud compute costs
project_goal.rationale: Manual FITS processing scripts require researcher availability overnight; automation enables unattended nightly processing

target_users.value: Astronomers and observatory technical staff managing nightly observation campaigns
target_users.rationale: Primary users check pipeline results each morning and approve catalog updates

project_class.rationale: Scheduled batch image processing pipeline; no user-facing UI required

capabilities.rationale: Data processing, scheduled batch execution, and telescope control system integration are the core capabilities

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-step calibration pipeline with source extraction is moderate complexity; no real-time requirements

risk_level.rationale: Internal-only research data with no sensitive PII or regulated content

data_sensitivity.rationale: Telescope observation data is internal research data; no regulated sensitivity classification

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline operates on observatory internal network; no public API surface

access_model.value: No public auth surface; pipeline triggered by telescope shutdown signal; astronomer access to outputs via shared S3 bucket with IAM roles
access_model.rationale: Pipeline is fully automated; researchers access outputs through existing IAM-controlled S3 paths

feature_scope.value: FITS file ingestion from telescope control system, bias/dark/flat-field calibration frames application, reduced image generation, source extraction with catalog update, S3 Glacier archival for long-term storage, CloudWatch observability
feature_scope.rationale: Full automated replacement of manual nightly processing steps

mvp_scope.value: FITS retrieval, calibration frame application, reduced image output to S3, basic source extraction with new detection flagging for astronomer review
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum to validate automated pipeline replaces manual nightly scripts

security_baseline.value: S3 bucket with IAM role-based access, no public S3 URLs, CloudTrail logging for S3 data access, EC2 spot instance termination handling with checkpoint saves
security_baseline.rationale: Protect research data from unauthorized access; handle spot interruptions gracefully

frontend_stack.value: None - data pipeline with no UI; results accessible via S3 paths
frontend_stack.rationale: No frontend required; astronomers use existing tools (DS9, TOPCAT) to open output files

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Prefect 3.x for orchestration; astropy and sep for FITS processing and source extraction
backend_stack.rationale: Prefect provides lightweight pipeline orchestration; astropy is the astronomy community standard for FITS handling

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS for observation metadata and catalog; S3 for FITS file storage; S3 Glacier for long-term archive
data_platform.rationale: RDS for queryable catalog metadata; S3 for large FITS binary files; Glacier for cost-optimized archive

hosting_target.value: EC2 Spot Instances (c5.4xlarge) for compute-intensive calibration; Prefect agent on reserved t3.medium for orchestration
hosting_target.rationale: Spot instances reduce calibration compute costs by ~70%; reserved t3.medium for persistent Prefect agent

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw FITS files retained 2 years on S3 Standard, then transitioned to Glacier for indefinite archive; reduced images and catalogs retained indefinitely; pipeline logs retained 90 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Observatory data sharing commitments require multi-year retention; Glacier minimizes archive costs

future_scope.value: Automated transient detection alerts, integration with Virtual Observatory (VO) protocols for data sharing, ML-based artifact classification
future_scope.rationale: Research center roadmap for science-enabling features after pipeline stabilization

constraints.value: Pipeline must complete before astronomer morning check-in (06:00 local); total cloud spend under $500/month; no GPU instances
constraints.rationale: Research budget and astronomer workflow constraints

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch metrics and logs; email SNS alert on pipeline failure; Prefect UI for flow run status
observability_baseline.rationale: Observatory staff check Prefect UI each morning; SNS email for overnight failures

execution_preference.value: GitHub Actions for CI; Prefect Cloud for orchestration UI; nightly trigger at telescope shutdown
execution_preference.rationale: Prefect Cloud provides hosted UI without self-managed infrastructure

project.goal: Automated nightly telescope FITS image calibration and archival pipeline
project.target_users: Astronomers and observatory staff
project.timeline: Q2 deployment for next observation season

backend.api: Internal flow tasks; no external API

security.auth: IAM roles for S3 access; no public endpoints

data.storage: S3 for FITS files, S3 Glacier for archive

devops.hosting: EC2 Spot c5.4xlarge + Prefect agent on t3.medium

reasoner_reviews.product: Pipeline scope covers the core manual steps astronomers currently run overnight. Source extraction with catalog update is the key science output. The morning review workflow is well-supported by Prefect UI flow run status.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Prefect with EC2 spot is the right cost/capability tradeoff. Checkpoint saves before spot termination prevent losing calibration work in progress. The pipeline is sequential enough that a single Prefect flow with tasks per observation night is appropriate; no need for complex DAG fanout.
reasoner_reviews.security: S3 IAM role access without public URLs is sufficient for internal research data. Spot termination handling is critical to avoid corrupted partial calibration outputs being written to S3.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Monthly cost estimate under $500 is achievable with spot pricing at c5.4xlarge ~$0.08/hour. A 3-hour nightly run costs ~$0.24/night or ~$7/month for compute; remaining budget covers RDS and S3.

backend.service_design: Single Prefect flow per observation night: NightlyCalibrationFlow with tasks IngestFITSTask, ApplyCalibrationTask, ReduceImageTask, ExtractSourcesTask, UpdateCatalogTask, ArchiveToGlacierTask. Spot interruption handled by Prefect task state checkpointing to S3 before each task completes.
backend.background_jobs: Prefect agent on reserved t3.medium polls Prefect Cloud for scheduled flow runs. Telescope shutdown signal triggers flow run submission via Prefect API webhook.
backend.failure_handling: Prefect retry with 2 attempts for transient S3 read/write failures. Spot interruption signal handler saves task checkpoint to S3 before termination. Failed flow run sends SNS email to observatory staff.

data.entities: observation_nights (id, date, telescope_id, seeing_arcsec, weather_conditions, raw_fits_count, pipeline_status ENUM pending/running/complete/failed, completed_at). raw_images (id, observation_night_id, fits_filename, s3_key, filter_band, exposure_seconds, object_name, ra_deg, dec_deg, ingested_at). calibration_frames (id, frame_type ENUM bias/dark/flat, filter_band, fits_filename, s3_key, valid_from date, valid_to date). reduced_images (id, raw_image_id, s3_key, calibration_frame_ids JSON, reduction_at, wcs_solved boolean). catalog_entries (id, observation_night_id, ra_deg, dec_deg, mag_inst, mag_error, source_fwhm, is_new_detection boolean, cross_match_id). pipeline_runs (id, observation_night_id, prefect_flow_run_id, started_at, completed_at, status, error_detail).
data.storage_design: S3 bucket s3://observatory-data/raw/{date}/{filter}/ for raw FITS files. s3://observatory-data/reduced/{date}/ for calibration outputs. S3 Glacier via lifecycle policy after 2 years. RDS PostgreSQL for catalog_entries and observation metadata.
data.retention_and_deletion: Raw FITS S3 Standard 2 years then Glacier indefinite. Reduced images 5 years S3 Standard then Glacier. Catalog entries retained indefinitely in RDS. Pipeline logs 90 days CloudWatch.

devops.deployment_topology: Prefect agent on t3.medium reserved instance. EC2 spot c5.4xlarge launched per flow run via Prefect ECSTask infrastructure block. Terraform manages RDS, S3 buckets, EC2 IAM roles.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions on PR: pylint, pytest unit tests for calibration math and source extraction logic. Deploy: push updated flow code to S3 flows bucket; Prefect agent picks up new version on next run.
devops.cost_controls: EC2 spot with interruption handler. S3 Intelligent-Tiering not used; explicit lifecycle rules for Glacier transition. Reserved t3.medium for Prefect agent. RDS db.t3.micro sufficient for catalog query volume.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a cost-optimized Prefect pipeline on AWS for nightly telescope FITS calibration. Key decisions: EC2 spot for compute savings with checkpoint-based interruption handling, astropy/sep for FITS and source extraction, RDS for queryable catalog metadata, S3 lifecycle to Glacier for long-term archive. Plan quality is weak: sections meet minimums but lack depth in several areas.
target_output.title: Nightly Telescope Observation Image Processing Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A Prefect 3.x pipeline on AWS that automatically ingests raw FITS observation files from the telescope control system after each nightly shutdown, applies bias, dark, and flat-field calibration frames, produces science-ready reduced images, runs source extraction to update the running object catalog, and archives outputs to S3 with Glacier lifecycle transitions for cost-optimized long-term storage.
target_output.architecture_overview: Prefect 3.x flow orchestrated from a persistent agent on a reserved t3.medium instance. Each observation night triggers NightlyCalibrationFlow dispatched to an EC2 Spot c5.4xlarge worker via Prefect ECSTask infrastructure block. Flow tasks process raw FITS files from S3, apply master calibration frames (bias, dark, flat), output reduced images back to S3, run sep source extraction to generate catalog_entries in RDS PostgreSQL, and trigger Glacier archival via S3 lifecycle. Spot interruption signal handler checkpoints task state to S3 before termination to enable resume without re-processing completed frames.
target_output.technology_stack: Orchestrator: Prefect 3.x with Prefect Cloud hosted UI. Language: Python 3.12. FITS processing: astropy 6.x (FITS I/O, WCS, header parsing), ccdproc 2.x (bias/dark/flat calibration), sep 1.x (source extraction). Database: RDS PostgreSQL 16 db.t3.micro for catalog and observation metadata. Storage: S3 for FITS files and reduced images; S3 Glacier via lifecycle rules. Compute: EC2 Spot c5.4xlarge. IaC: Terraform 1.8. CI/CD: GitHub Actions.
target_output.functional_feature_map: FITS ingestion: rsync or S3 sync from telescope control system staging directory to S3 raw bucket after shutdown signal. Calibration: ccdproc applies master bias (median-combine of bias frames), master dark (scaled to exposure time), and master flat (filter-matched) to each raw science frame. WCS solving: astrometry.net local solve on reduced images for coordinate calibration. Source extraction: sep detects sources above 5-sigma threshold, computes instrumental magnitudes and FWHM, writes to catalog_entries with is_new_detection flag for astronomer review. Catalog update: cross-match against existing catalog within 1.5 arcsec to link repeat detections. Archive: S3 lifecycle rule transitions raw FITS to Glacier after 2 years.
target_output.system_components: Prefect agent (t3.medium): polls Prefect Cloud, submits flow runs on telescope shutdown trigger. EC2 Spot worker (c5.4xlarge): runs NightlyCalibrationFlow tasks. S3 buckets: observatory-data/raw/ and observatory-data/reduced/ with lifecycle rules. RDS PostgreSQL: observation_nights, raw_images, calibration_frames, reduced_images, catalog_entries tables. CloudWatch: pipeline log groups, SNS alert on flow failure. Telescope shutdown webhook: HTTP trigger to Prefect Cloud API endpoint on telescope parking sequence.
target_output.workflows: Nightly calibration workflow: telescope control system sends HTTP webhook to Prefect Cloud API after parking sequence; NightlyCalibrationFlow run queued; spot worker launched; IngestFITSTask syncs raw FITS from staging to S3 and inserts raw_images records in RDS; ApplyCalibrationTask loads master calibration frames for the night filter set, applies ccdproc pipeline to each science frame, writes reduced FITS to S3 reduced/ path; ExtractSourcesTask runs sep on each reduced image, writes catalog_entries rows with is_new_detection=True for sources not matched in existing catalog; ArchiveCheck marks observation_night pipeline_status=complete. Morning review workflow: astronomer opens Prefect Cloud UI at 06:00, checks flow run status, queries RDS for new detections from last night, opens flagged reduced images in DS9 for visual inspection.
target_output.data_model: observation_nights (id UUID PK, date date, telescope_id, seeing_arcsec numeric, weather_conditions text, raw_fits_count integer, pipeline_status ENUM pending/running/complete/failed, completed_at timestamptz). raw_images (id UUID PK, observation_night_id FK, fits_filename, s3_key, filter_band ENUM B/V/R/I/Ha, exposure_seconds numeric, object_name, ra_deg numeric, dec_deg numeric, ingested_at timestamptz). calibration_frames (id UUID PK, frame_type ENUM bias/dark/flat, filter_band, s3_key, valid_from date, valid_to date). reduced_images (id UUID PK, raw_image_id FK, s3_key, reduction_at timestamptz, wcs_solved boolean, airmass numeric). catalog_entries (id UUID PK, observation_night_id FK, ra_deg numeric, dec_deg numeric, mag_inst numeric, mag_error numeric, source_fwhm_arcsec numeric, is_new_detection boolean, cross_match_catalog_id UUID nullable). pipeline_runs (id UUID PK, observation_night_id FK, prefect_flow_run_id, started_at, completed_at, status, error_detail text).
target_output.api_design: No external API surface. Internal Prefect task interfaces. IngestFITSTask(night_id: str, s3_staging_prefix: str) -> List[str] raw_image_ids. ApplyCalibrationTask(raw_image_ids: List[str], calibration_date: date) -> List[str] reduced_image_ids. ExtractSourcesTask(reduced_image_ids: List[str]) -> int new_detections_count. UpdateCatalogTask(reduced_image_ids: List[str]) -> None. ArchiveToGlacierTask not required; handled by S3 lifecycle rule. Telescope trigger: POST to Prefect Cloud flow run API with deployment_id and parameters {night_date, telescope_id}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: S3 buckets with IAM role-based access only; no public S3 bucket policies. Spot instance IAM role scoped to observatory-data S3 bucket and RDS connection via Secrets Manager. S3 server-side encryption SSE-S3 for raw and reduced FITS files. CloudTrail logging enabled on S3 data events for access audit. Spot interruption SIGTERM handler in Python catches signal, checkpoints current task progress to S3 state key, gracefully terminates. RDS access via Secrets Manager credential rotation every 90 days.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform manages RDS instance, S3 buckets with lifecycle rules, EC2 IAM roles, and CloudWatch log groups. GitHub Actions CI on PR: pylint, mypy, pytest unit tests for calibration pipeline math (mock astropy FITS fixtures) and source extraction threshold logic. Deploy: push updated Prefect flow code to S3 flows bucket and update Prefect Cloud deployment via prefect deploy CLI. Environments: dev uses small synthetic FITS test files; production uses real observatory data. No staging environment due to budget constraints.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch log groups for Prefect agent and spot worker task logs. CloudWatch metric alarm on NightlyCalibrationFlow failure triggers SNS email to observatory-ops@facility.edu. Prefect Cloud UI shows per-task run status, duration, and log output for each observation night. Key metrics tracked: pipeline_duration_minutes per night, new_detections_count per night, calibration_frame_age_days (alert if master calibration frames older than 30 days).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: EC2 spot c5.4xlarge at ~$0.08/hour; 3-hour nightly run ~$7/month. RDS db.t3.micro ~$15/month. S3 Standard ~$10/month at 2 TB observation data. Prefect Cloud free tier covers single workspace. Total ~$35/month well under $500 constraint. Glacier lifecycle transitions reduce long-term storage to ~$0.004/GB/month for archived raw FITS.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - MVP (6 weeks): FITS ingestion from S3 staging, bias/dark/flat calibration with ccdproc, reduced image output, basic source extraction with new detection flagging, RDS catalog loading, CloudWatch alerting. Phase 2 (4 weeks): WCS astrometric solving with astrometry.net local index, cross-match to existing catalog for repeat detection linking, calibration frame age validation pre-run check. Phase 3 (roadmap): Automated transient detection alerts, Virtual Observatory VOTable output for data sharing, ML artifact classification for cosmic ray rejection.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with type annotations on all task functions. pytest unit tests for calibration arithmetic and source extraction threshold logic using synthetic FITS fixtures generated with astropy. Spot interruption handler unit tested with mock SIGTERM. All S3 write operations use put_object with ChecksumSHA256 for data integrity verification. Calibration frame validity date ranges validated before pipeline start; if no valid master frame exists for a filter, flow fails fast with descriptive error rather than applying stale calibration.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: spot instance interruption during WCS solving (CPU-intensive, non-resumable mid-step). Mitigation: checkpoint reduced image list before WCS solving; on resume, skip images already solved. Risk: calibration frames becoming stale if observers skip calibration sequence. Mitigation: pipeline validates calibration frame valid_to date at run start; alerts if frames older than 30 days. Tradeoff: ccdproc CPU-only calibration over custom GPU pipeline accepts longer runtime (3h vs 45min) to avoid GPU instance costs within $500/month budget.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Orchestrator: Prefect over Airflow; lighter operational overhead for single-pipeline use case. Calibration library: ccdproc over custom NumPy pipeline for community-standard validation. S3 Glacier: lifecycle rule preferred over manual Glacier API calls for operational simplicity. WCS solving: astrometry.net local index in Phase 2 deferred from MVP to avoid index download complexity in initial deployment.

metadata.notes: Weak quality: several sections at minimum word counts; no staging environment due to budget constraints explicitly noted.

profile.domain: Particle Physics Experiment Real-time Sensor Telemetry Infrastructure

project_goal.value: Real-time sensor telemetry infrastructure for a large-scale particle physics experiment, ingesting high-frequency detector readouts from hundreds of sensor nodes, streaming data to trigger/analysis workers, and providing low-latency monitoring dashboards for experiment operators
project_goal.rationale: Replace ad-hoc data acquisition scripts with production-grade infrastructure supporting experiment runs at design luminosity

target_users.value: Experiment operators monitoring live detector health, physics analysts consuming trigger-selected event data, DAQ engineers maintaining data acquisition systems
target_users.rationale: Three distinct user personas with different latency and throughput requirements

project_class.rationale: Platform and networking infrastructure project provisioning Kafka, Kubernetes, InfluxDB, and custom DAQ framework - not an application

capabilities.rationale: Streaming ingest, time-series data, infrastructure provisioning, real-time processing, and operator analytics are the core capabilities

complexity_level.rationale: 1 MHz aggregate throughput from hundreds of detector nodes with trigger latency requirements makes this high-scale infrastructure

risk_level.rationale: Internal experimental network, no public exposure, but infrastructure failure during a physics run has significant scientific cost

data_sensitivity.rationale: Detector readout and physics event data is proprietary research data; no regulated sensitivity classification

external_exposure.rationale: Entire infrastructure runs on isolated experiment intranet; no public internet exposure

access_model.value: Kubernetes RBAC for cluster access; Kafka ACLs per consumer group; Grafana with LDAP experiment directory authentication; no public endpoints
access_model.rationale: Experiment collaboration uses central LDAP directory; Kafka ACLs prevent unauthorized consumer groups from accessing event streams

feature_scope.value: Kafka cluster for high-throughput detector readout streaming, custom C++ DAQ framework nodes publishing to Kafka, Flink trigger workers consuming sensor streams and selecting physics events, InfluxDB for time-series detector health metrics, Grafana operator dashboards with per-node hit rates and alarm escalation, permanent event storage to CERN EOS or local RAID arrays
feature_scope.rationale: Full infrastructure stack for production experiment data acquisition

mvp_scope.value: Kafka cluster with detector readout ingest, Flink trigger worker with configurable threshold algorithms, InfluxDB detector health metrics, Grafana operator dashboard with alarm escalation for hit rate and noise anomalies
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum infrastructure to support commissioning runs with real detector data before design luminosity

security_baseline.value: Kubernetes RBAC, Kafka TLS between brokers and producers/consumers, Kafka ACLs per consumer group, no external network routes from experiment cluster, audit logging for Kubernetes API server
security_baseline.rationale: Prevent unauthorized data access or configuration changes during live physics runs

frontend_stack.value: None - infrastructure_project; operator dashboards via Grafana (not custom-built)
frontend_stack.rationale: Grafana is the standard physics experiment monitoring tool; no custom frontend development required

backend_stack.value: C++ with custom DAQ framework for detector node publishers; Apache Flink 1.19 for stream processing trigger workers; Python 3.12 for infrastructure tooling and Prometheus exporters
backend_stack.rationale: C++ DAQ framework is standard in high-energy physics for deterministic low-latency data acquisition; Flink for stateful trigger algorithms on Kafka streams

data_platform.value: Apache Kafka (5-broker KRaft mode cluster) for detector event streams; InfluxDB 2.x for time-series detector health metrics; Redis 7 for per-node state cache; local RAID array for selected event permanent storage
data_platform.rationale: Kafka for high-throughput durable streaming; InfluxDB for nanosecond-precision time-series; Redis for fast per-node counter state

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal Kubernetes (k3s) on experiment intranet servers in underground detector hall and surface control room
hosting_target.rationale: Bare-metal for deterministic network latency; no cloud dependencies in underground environment with limited connectivity

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw detector readout streams retained in Kafka for 24 hours; selected physics events on RAID indefinitely; detector health metrics in InfluxDB for 1 year then purged; run configuration retained indefinitely in version control
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Raw streams transient for reprocessing window; selected events are scientific record; metric history needed for detector characterization

future_scope.value: GPU-accelerated online reconstruction workers, federated data transfer to CERN Grid for off-site analysis, automated run quality assessment ML pipeline
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 science capabilities after commissioning infrastructure stabilizes

constraints.value: Must sustain 1 MHz aggregate readout throughput; trigger latency under 10 ms; no cloud connectivity during underground runs; bare-metal k3s only
constraints.rationale: Physics run requirements and underground connectivity constraints

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards on Prometheus metrics; InfluxDB for detector time-series; PagerDuty-equivalent on-call alert system for experiment operators; Jenkins for infrastructure CI
observability_baseline.rationale: Experiment operations require 24/7 shift coverage with alarm escalation for detector anomalies

execution_preference.value: Terraform + Helm for infrastructure-as-code; Jenkins pipeline for DAQ software build and deployment; Ansible for bare-metal node provisioning
execution_preference.rationale: Physics lab standard toolchain for reproducible experiment configuration management

project.goal: Production real-time detector telemetry and event selection infrastructure for particle physics experiment
project.target_users: Experiment operators, physics analysts, DAQ engineers

backend.runtime: C++ DAQ framework + Apache Flink 1.19 + Python 3.12 tooling
backend.framework: Custom DAQ publishers, Flink trigger workers, Prometheus exporters
backend.api: Kafka topics for all data flows; no HTTP API surface

security.auth: Kubernetes RBAC, Kafka ACLs, Grafana LDAP
security.transport: Kafka inter-broker TLS, mTLS for producer authentication

reasoner_reviews.product: Infrastructure scope is correctly focused on the commissioning MVP: Kafka ingest, Flink trigger, InfluxDB metrics, Grafana dashboards. GPU reconstruction and CERN Grid transfer are appropriately deferred. The 1 MHz throughput and 10 ms trigger latency requirements are design constraints that must be validated during load testing before the first physics run.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Kafka KRaft 5-broker cluster is the right choice for 1 MHz aggregate at reliable sub-millisecond per-message latency. Flink stateful stream processing with Kafka consumer groups per trigger algorithm allows multiple concurrent trigger implementations to run in parallel without impacting each other. InfluxDB 2.x with nanosecond timestamps is appropriate for detector health metrics where sub-millisecond event timing matters. Redis for per-node hit rate counters avoids InfluxDB write amplification at high-frequency polling.
reasoner_reviews.security: Kafka mTLS for producer authentication is important to prevent rogue devices from injecting fabricated detector data into the trigger stream. Kubernetes RBAC must restrict Flink worker pod service accounts to read-only access on detector-readout topics and write access on trigger-output topics only.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Bare-metal k3s on experiment intranet eliminates cloud latency variability. 10 ms trigger latency at 1 MHz requires careful Kafka partition count tuning and Flink parallelism configuration during commissioning. Network topology placing Kafka brokers close to detector hall nodes on dedicated 10GbE switches is critical.

backend.service_design: DAQ layer: C++ detector node processes running on embedded readout boards publish raw sensor_readouts to Kafka topic detector-readout (128 partitions, partitioned by detector_node_id). Trigger layer: Flink jobs consume detector-readout, implement configurable trigger algorithms (coincidence trigger, energy threshold trigger), publish selected events to trigger-output topic. Slow control layer: Python Prometheus exporters scrape per-node counters from Redis and expose /metrics for Grafana scraping.
backend.api_patterns: All data flows via Kafka topics: detector-readout (raw readouts, 24h retention), trigger-output (selected events, written to RAID), detector-health (InfluxDB line protocol from Python exporters), run-config (run configuration changes, broadcast to all workers). No HTTP API surface in critical data path.
backend.failure_handling: Kafka broker failure: KRaft 5-broker cluster tolerates 2 broker failures without data loss (replication factor 3). Flink job failure: Flink checkpointing to HDFS-compatible NFS every 30s; job restarts from checkpoint. Detector node disconnect: Kafka consumer group rebalances within 30s; Redis hit-rate counter for disconnected node zeroed and alarm raised.
backend.scaling_notes: 128 Kafka partitions on detector-readout support up to 128 parallel Flink consumer tasks. Flink parallelism set to 64 for MVP; scale to 128 for full detector commissioning. Redis cluster-mode 3 shards for per-node counter state at 500 node count.

data.entities: detector_nodes (node_id string, detector_subsystem ENUM tracker/calorimeter/muon_system, location_string, expected_hit_rate_hz, alarm_threshold_hz_low, alarm_threshold_hz_high, status ENUM active/disconnected/maintenance). sensor_readouts (Kafka message: node_id, timestamp_ns int64, channel_id uint16, adc_value uint16, trigger_flag boolean). trigger_events (Kafka message: event_id uuid, trigger_type ENUM coincidence/energy_threshold, timestamp_ns, contributing_node_ids JSON, raw_payload_bytes blob written to RAID). run_configs (id, run_number, start_time, stop_time, trigger_algorithm_config JSONB, detector_node_ids JSON, status ENUM planned/running/complete). alarm_thresholds (node_id FK, metric_name, low_threshold, high_threshold, alert_severity ENUM warning/critical).
data.storage_design: Kafka topic detector-readout: 128 partitions, replication factor 3, 24h log retention, 1 TB log segment size. InfluxDB measurement detector_health: tags node_id, subsystem; fields hit_rate_hz, noise_rate_hz, dead_channel_count, temperature_c; timestamp nanosecond precision; 1-year retention policy. Redis: per-node hit_rate_hz and dead_channel_count counters with 10s TTL updated by Prometheus exporter. RAID array: trigger-selected events in ROOT TTree format by run_number, retained indefinitely.
data.consistency_notes: Kafka exactly-once semantics enabled on trigger-output producer. Flink savepoints taken before run configuration changes. Redis TTL expiry handled gracefully by exporter - expired key returns zero, triggering disconnected-node alarm path.

devops.deployment_topology: Underground detector hall: 3 Kafka brokers on Dell R750 servers on dedicated 10GbE switch. Surface control room: 2 Kafka brokers (async replica), Flink cluster (1 JobManager + 4 TaskManagers on 48-core servers), InfluxDB, Redis cluster, Grafana. k3s cluster spanning both locations via 10GbE fiber link.
devops.ci_cd_design: Jenkins pipeline: C++ DAQ code cmake build and unit tests (Google Test), Python linting and pytest for exporters, Helm chart lint, Terraform plan validation. Deployment: Helm upgrade for Kafka/Flink/InfluxDB chart versions; kubectl apply for Flink job JAR deployments. Ansible playbooks for bare-metal node OS provisioning and k3s join.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scrapes Kafka JMX exporter, Flink metrics API, custom Python detector exporters. Grafana dashboards: per-node hit rate heatmap (color-coded by subsystem), dead channel map, Flink job throughput and lag, Kafka consumer group lag per topic. InfluxDB stores 1-year detector health history for characterization studies.
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm rollback to previous chart version for Kafka/Flink components. Flink savepoints allow job rollback to last checkpoint before failed deployment. Terraform state stored on NFS to allow plan/apply rollback.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing high-scale bare-metal Kafka/Flink/InfluxDB/Kubernetes infrastructure for a particle physics detector at 1 MHz aggregate throughput and 10 ms trigger latency. Key decisions: KRaft 5-broker Kafka with 128 partitions on dedicated 10GbE switches for detector proximity, Flink stateful stream processing for configurable trigger algorithms, InfluxDB nanosecond time-series for detector health, Redis for high-frequency per-node counter state. No cloud dependencies; bare-metal k3s spanning underground and surface locations.
target_output.title: Particle Physics Experiment Real-time Detector Telemetry Infrastructure
target_output.executive_summary: A bare-metal Kubernetes (k3s) infrastructure platform spanning underground detector hall and surface control room, providing a 5-broker Kafka KRaft cluster at 1 MHz aggregate detector readout throughput, Apache Flink stream processing for configurable physics trigger algorithms with sub-10ms latency, InfluxDB nanosecond time-series for detector health monitoring, and Grafana dashboards with alarm escalation for 24/7 experiment operator shift coverage.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier infrastructure architecture on isolated experiment intranet. DAQ tier: C++ detector node processes on embedded readout boards publish raw sensor_readouts to Kafka topic detector-readout (128 partitions, keyed by detector_node_id) via Kafka mTLS producers over dedicated 10GbE switches co-located with detector hardware in the underground hall. Processing tier: Apache Flink cluster on surface control room servers consumes detector-readout with parallelism 64, implements coincidence and energy-threshold trigger algorithms as stateful Flink jobs, publishes selected trigger_events to trigger-output topic and ROOT TTree files on local RAID. Monitoring tier: Python Prometheus exporters scrape per-node hit-rate counters from Redis, expose /metrics to Prometheus, visualized in Grafana dashboards with alarm escalation. All tiers provisioned via Terraform, Helm, and Ansible on bare-metal k3s.
target_output.technology_stack: Message broker: Apache Kafka 3.7 KRaft mode (5 brokers, 128 partitions). Stream processor: Apache Flink 1.19 with Kafka connector. DAQ framework: C++ with librdkafka for Kafka producer. Time-series: InfluxDB 2.7 with Flux queries. Cache: Redis 7 cluster (3 shards). Monitoring: Prometheus 2.x, Grafana 10.x, Kafka JMX exporter. IaC: Terraform 1.8 for k3s node config, Helm 3.x for application charts, Ansible 2.17 for bare-metal provisioning. Data: ROOT TTree format for permanent event storage. CI/CD: Jenkins 2.x.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Detector readout ingest: C++ DAQ publishers encode raw ADC readouts with nanosecond timestamps into Kafka messages on detector-readout topic; 128 partitions support 1 MHz aggregate from up to 512 detector nodes at 2 kHz per node. Physics trigger processing: Flink jobs implement configurable trigger algorithms reading from detector-readout - coincidence trigger selects events with hits in 3 or more subsystems within 5 ns window; energy threshold trigger selects calorimeter deposits above configurable GeV threshold; selected events published to trigger-output. Detector health monitoring: Python exporters poll Redis counters updated by DAQ nodes (hit_rate_hz, dead_channel_count per node), write to InfluxDB detector_health measurement, expose Prometheus metrics. Operator dashboards: Grafana visualizes live per-node hit rate heatmap by subsystem, dead channel maps, Flink consumer group lag, Kafka broker throughput. Alarm escalation: Prometheus AlertManager sends shift-operator page when node hit rate deviates more than 20% from run configuration baseline or dead channel count exceeds threshold.
target_output.system_components: Kafka KRaft cluster (5 nodes, 3 in underground hall, 2 surface async replica): detector-readout topic 128 partitions replication-factor 3, trigger-output topic 32 partitions replication-factor 3. Flink cluster: 1 JobManager, 4 TaskManagers (48 vCPU each), parallelism 64; CoincidenceTriggerJob and EnergyThresholdTriggerJob deployed as separate JARs. C++ DAQ Publisher: librdkafka Kafka producer per detector readout board, mTLS certificate per node. Redis cluster: 3 shards storing hit_rate_hz and dead_channel_count per node_id with 10s TTL. InfluxDB: detector_health measurement, nanosecond precision, 1-year retention. Prometheus + Alertmanager: scrapes Kafka JMX, Flink metrics API, Python /metrics endpoints; routes alarms to shift operator on-call roster. Grafana: 4 pre-built dashboards - Live Detector Status, Run Quality Overview, Infrastructure Health, Trigger Rate History. RAID array: trigger-selected event storage in ROOT TTree files partitioned by run_number.
target_output.workflows: Physics run startup workflow: shift operator updates run_configs via Grafana-linked run control panel, broadcasts run_number and trigger_algorithm_config to run-config Kafka topic; Flink jobs consume run-config and reload trigger thresholds without restart; DAQ operators verify all detector_nodes show active status in Redis; operator approves run start in run control system. Live detector monitoring workflow: Prometheus scrapes Python exporters every 5 seconds, updating Grafana hit-rate heatmap; if node hit_rate_hz drops below alarm_threshold_hz_low, AlertManager fires warning alert to shift operator Slack channel; if hit_rate_hz reaches zero, critical alert fires to on-call pager. Trigger event selection workflow: Flink CoincidenceTriggerJob reads batches of sensor_readouts from detector-readout, uses Flink event-time processing with 5 ns watermark window, identifies coincident multi-subsystem hit clusters, serializes selected trigger_events to trigger-output topic and ROOT TTree writer task writing to RAID at run_number partition.
target_output.data_model: detector_nodes (node_id string PK, detector_subsystem ENUM tracker/calorimeter/muon_system/forward_spectrometer, location string, expected_hit_rate_hz float, alarm_low_hz float, alarm_high_hz float, status ENUM active/disconnected/maintenance, last_heartbeat_at timestamptz). sensor_readouts (Kafka schema: node_id string, timestamp_ns int64, channel_id uint16, adc_value uint16, trigger_flag boolean - 128-byte fixed message). trigger_events (Kafka + RAID: event_id uuid, run_number int, trigger_type ENUM coincidence/energy_threshold, event_timestamp_ns int64, hit_count int, contributing_nodes string[], reconstructed_energy_gev float optional, raw_payload bytes written to ROOT TTree). run_configs (id uuid, run_number int, start_time timestamptz, stop_time, trigger_algorithm_config JSONB with algorithm name and threshold parameters, detector_node_ids string[], beam_energy_gev, status ENUM planned/running/complete). Redis keys: node:{node_id}:hit_rate_hz float, node:{node_id}:dead_channel_count int, TTL 10s.
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API surface in critical data path. All data flows via Kafka topics. DAQ publisher: librdkafka C++ client produces to detector-readout with exactly-once enabled, partition key = node_id hash. Flink consumer: CoincidenceTriggerJob consumer group coincidence-trigger-v1, EnergyThresholdTriggerJob consumer group energy-trigger-v1 - separate groups allow independent lag tracking. Run control: run-config Kafka topic (1 partition, compact) for broadcast of RunConfigMessage protobuf to all workers. Infrastructure management: kubectl and Helm CLI for operator access with Kubernetes RBAC. Grafana provisioned datasources: Prometheus and InfluxDB - no manual configuration required after Helm install.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Kafka mTLS: each DAQ detector node has a unique TLS client certificate issued by experiment CA; Kafka ACL restricts each node certificate to produce-only on detector-readout for its assigned partition range. Kafka consumer ACLs: Flink service account read-only on detector-readout, write-only on trigger-output. Kubernetes RBAC: DAQ engineer role restricted to DAQ namespace; physics analyst role read-only on trigger-output namespace. Grafana LDAP authentication against experiment collaboration LDAP directory; viewer role for analysts, editor for shift operators. k3s API server audit logging enabled, logs shipped to Prometheus Loki sidecar. No external network routes from experiment cluster - ingress controller disabled, all services ClusterIP.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Ansible playbooks provision bare-metal nodes: OS (Rocky Linux 9), k3s install, 10GbE interface configuration, NTP synchronization to experiment GPS clock. Terraform provisions k3s cluster namespaces, Kafka PersistentVolumes on local SSD, InfluxDB PV on RAID, Grafana ConfigMaps for dashboard provisioning. Helm charts deploy Kafka (Bitnami chart with KRaft values override), Flink (operator chart), InfluxDB (official chart), Grafana, Prometheus stack. Jenkins pipeline: cmake build of C++ DAQ code, Google Test unit tests, Helm lint and Terraform validate, Flink JAR build via Maven. Deploy: Jenkins promotes Flink JARs and Helm chart versions to production namespace via Helm upgrade --atomic. Flink savepoints captured via Jenkins pre-deploy step before any Flink job upgrade.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes: Kafka JMX exporter (broker throughput, under-replicated partitions, consumer group lag per topic), Flink metrics API (job throughput msg/s, checkpoint duration, operator backpressure), Python exporter /metrics (per-node hit_rate_hz, dead_channel_count from Redis). InfluxDB detector_health measurement stores 1 year of nanosecond-precision per-node metrics for post-run detector characterization. Grafana dashboards: Live Detector Status (per-node hit rate heatmap, color-coded alarm state), Run Quality (trigger rate vs beam intensity, trigger efficiency), Infrastructure Health (Kafka broker state, Flink job status, Redis memory). AlertManager routes: hit rate deviation >20% -> Slack #shift-alarms; dead channels >threshold -> on-call page; Kafka under-replicated partitions -> DAQ engineer PagerDuty.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Infrastructure is on-premises bare-metal; no cloud compute costs. Hardware acquisition: 5 Kafka broker servers (Dell R750, ~$15K each), 4 Flink TaskManager servers (48-core ~$20K each), 3 Redis nodes (~$5K each). One-time capital cost ~$180K. Operational cost is electricity and facility overhead. Scaling path: Kafka partitions expand from 128 to 256 by adding partition replicas before full-luminosity run; Flink parallelism increases from 64 to 128 by adding TaskManager pods on existing hardware.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Commissioning infrastructure (12 weeks): Kafka 5-broker KRaft cluster deployment and load testing to 1 MHz synthetic readout, Flink CoincidenceTriggerJob with configurable coincidence window, InfluxDB and Grafana operator dashboards with per-node hit rate and alarm escalation, DAQ publisher library integration with 3 pilot detector node readout boards. Phase 2 - Full detector commissioning (8 weeks): All detector subsystem DAQ publishers integrated, EnergyThresholdTriggerJob deployed alongside coincidence trigger, RAID ROOT TTree event writer at full trigger rate, Prometheus AlertManager on-call escalation configured per shift roster. Phase 3 (roadmap): GPU reconstruction workers as Flink processing stage, CERN Grid data transfer integration, automated run quality ML assessment.
target_output.development_guidelines: C++ DAQ publisher code built with CMake, tested with Google Test including mock Kafka producer for unit tests without live cluster. Kafka message schema versioned with protobuf; schema changes require broker-side schema registry registration before deploying new producer version. Flink trigger algorithms must pass load test at 1.2x design throughput before production deployment. Terraform changes require peer review; Helm chart upgrades tested in dev k3s cluster (single-node laptop) before production. All infrastructure changes during a physics run are frozen; maintenance windows are run gaps in the beam schedule.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Underground Kafka broker network partition from surface during run. Mitigation: 3 underground brokers form quorum; surface brokers are async replicas only; trigger workers on surface can continue consuming from underground brokers during surface network loss. Risk: Flink trigger latency exceeding 10 ms at 1 MHz under backpressure. Mitigation: Flink backpressure monitoring dashboard; if avg latency exceeds 8 ms, auto-scale TaskManager replicas within available hardware. Tradeoff: ROOT TTree format for permanent storage over Parquet accepts physics-specific tooling dependency in exchange for direct compatibility with existing collaboration analysis code. Tradeoff: Redis for hit-rate counters over InfluxDB write path accepts 10s TTL data loss risk on Redis failure in exchange for 10x lower write amplification at 500 Hz counter update frequency.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Kafka vs NATS for streaming: Kafka chosen for durable log retention (24h replay window) and mature Flink connector over NATS lighter weight. Flink vs Spark Streaming for trigger: Flink event-time processing with nanosecond watermarks is more appropriate than Spark micro-batch for physics trigger latency requirements. Event storage format: ROOT TTree over custom binary for direct compatibility with collaboration ROOT analysis frameworks. Trigger algorithm deployment: separate Flink jobs per algorithm allow independent scaling and deployment without affecting other trigger streams.

metadata.notes: Strong infrastructure_project for particle physics experiment; bare-metal Kafka/Flink/InfluxDB at 1 MHz detector throughput with comprehensive operator monitoring.

project_goal.value: A data-rich public landing page for a scientific data repository platform showcasing dataset volume, domain diversity, recent depositions, and usage metrics to drive researcher sign-ups
project_goal.rationale: Primary conversion goal is researcher registration with evidence of dataset richness as the hook

target_users.value: Academic researchers, data scientists, and computational scientists considering depositing or accessing scientific datasets
target_users.rationale: Users are technically sophisticated and respond to concrete data volume and domain breadth

project_class.rationale: Static or edge-rendered page with no persistent server-side state; primary concern is performance and conversion

capabilities.rationale: Static content rendering and visitor behaviour analytics are the only runtime requirements

complexity_level.rationale: No authentication, database, or server-side logic; complexity is in data visualization and performance

risk_level.rationale: Public page with no user data collection beyond anonymized analytics

data_sensitivity.rationale: No personal or sensitive data processed; only aggregate repository statistics embedded at build time

external_exposure.rationale: Public marketing page with no access restrictions

access_model.value: Fully public with no authentication; GDPR-compliant cookie consent for EU visitors before analytics activation
access_model.rationale: Open access required for marketing reach; GDPR mandates consent for analytics

feature_scope.value: Domain dataset count visualizations by category, recent depositions feed, usage metrics (downloads, citations), researcher sign-up CTA, animated statistics counters, mobile-responsive layout
feature_scope.rationale: Data richness visualized on arrival drives sign-up intent

mvp_scope.value: Static page with embedded repository stats, domain distribution chart, recent depositions list, and sign-up CTA
mvp_scope.rationale: Core value proposition deliverable in 3 weeks without CMS integration

security_baseline.value: HTTPS enforced via Netlify CDN, CSP headers blocking inline scripts except analytics, no server-side secrets
security_baseline.rationale: Standard static site security posture

frontend_stack.value: Astro 4 with React islands and TypeScript for interactive visualization components
frontend_stack.rationale: Astro delivers zero-JS by default for static sections; React islands for D3-powered charts; excellent Netlify build integration

backend_stack.value: None - all data embedded at build time via Astro build-time fetch from repository stats API
backend_stack.rationale: No runtime backend required; stats refreshed on scheduled rebuilds

data_platform.value: None - repository aggregate stats fetched from public stats API at build time and embedded in static HTML
data_platform.rationale: Static data eliminates runtime DB dependency and attack surface

hosting_target.value: Netlify CDN with edge delivery
hosting_target.rationale: Zero-config static hosting with automatic previews per PR and 99.99% uptime SLA

privacy_retention_policy.value: No personal data retained; Plausible Analytics collects anonymized page view events with 12-month data retention; GDPR cookie consent banner required
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Privacy-preserving analytics avoids GDPR complexity

future_scope.value: Integration with headless CMS for marketing team self-service content updates, A/B testing for CTA variants, personalised domain-interest routing
future_scope.rationale: Post-launch conversion optimization roadmap

constraints.value: Build time must remain under 2 minutes; repository stats API rate-limited to 60 requests/hour; page must score >90 on Core Web Vitals
constraints.rationale: Performance SLA and API constraint define build architecture

observability_baseline.value: Plausible Analytics for visitor behaviour, Netlify build status badges, Lighthouse CI in PR pipeline for CWV regression detection
observability_baseline.rationale: Lightweight observability appropriate for static marketing page

execution_preference.value: 3-week sprint to MVP, continuous deployment on main branch merge
execution_preference.rationale: Marketing launch deadline drives timeline

project.goal: Data-rich scientific repository landing page driving researcher sign-ups
project.target_users: Academic researchers and computational scientists
project.timeline: MVP in 3 weeks, continuous deployment thereafter

frontend.charts: D3.js v7 for domain distribution and usage trend visualizations
frontend.approach: Static generation with React island hydration for interactive charts

security.headers: CSP, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy via netlify.toml headers config
security.analytics: Plausible Analytics with GDPR consent gate

data.source: Repository stats API polled at Astro build time
data.format: JSON embedded into page as static props

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for Lighthouse CI on PR, merge triggers Netlify auto-deploy
devops.environments: Netlify branch previews for PRs, production on main

reasoner_reviews.product: The data-richness-first narrative is correctly modeled for an academic audience that responds to evidence over marketing copy. Domain distribution chart and recent depositions feed are the right conversion hooks. GDPR consent before Plausible activation is correct given EU researcher audience.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro with React islands is the optimal choice: zero-JS for the static sections improves CWV LCP, and D3 chart islands hydrate only when scrolled into view via Astro partial hydration. Build-time fetch from the repository stats API is cleaner than client-side calls that would expose the API endpoint. Netlify scheduled builds every 6 hours keeps stats fresh without real-time complexity.
reasoner_reviews.security: CSP headers should explicitly whitelist Plausible analytics domain and block all inline scripts. No server-side secrets means no .env risk, but build-time API keys for the stats endpoint must be stored in Netlify environment variables and not committed to the repository.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 2-minute build constraint is achievable with a single stats API call returning pre-aggregated JSON. D3 chart island bundle size must be monitored; tree-shake D3 imports to avoid adding >30KB to the island bundle, which would risk CWV scores.

frontend.app_structure: Astro 4 project with src/pages/index.astro as the sole page, src/components/islands/ for React hydration islands, src/layouts/MarketingLayout.astro for shared head and consent banner
frontend.pages_and_flows: Single-page layout: Hero with animated dataset count (Astro static), DomainDistributionChart (React island, client:visible), RecentDepositions feed (Astro static list from build data), UsageMetricsRow (Astro static counters), ResearcherSignupCTA (React island with form posting to repository API)
frontend.performance_notes: client:visible hydration strategy for all React islands avoids JS execution until element is scrolled into view; D3 imports tree-shaken to only selection, scaleOrdinal, arc, pie modules; images served as WebP with explicit width/height to eliminate CLS; Netlify automatic image CDN for any researcher profile images
frontend.frontend_risks: D3 bundle bloat if full library imported; mitigated by named imports. CLS from fonts if not preloaded; mitigated by font-display: swap and preload link tags.

security.auth_design: None required - fully public page
security.abuse_prevention: CSP header blocks all inline scripts and restricts script-src to self and Plausible CDN domain; Netlify bot protection enabled
security.privacy_controls: GDPR cookie consent banner implemented as React island; Plausible analytics script injected only after explicit consent; consent stored in localStorage with 1-year expiry

data.entities: dataset_stats (total_count INT, by_domain JSONB array of {domain_name, count, color_hex}, updated_at), recent_depositions (array of {title, domain, deposited_at, doi, size_gb}), usage_metrics (total_downloads INT, total_citations INT, active_researchers INT)
data.storage_design: No database; all entities fetched from repository stats API at Astro build time via getStaticProps equivalent, serialized to JSON, and embedded in static HTML as data attributes consumed by React islands
data.analytics_events: page_view (Plausible default), cta_click (custom event with variant label), domain_filter_interaction (custom event with domain name selected in chart)

devops.deployment_topology: Netlify CDN with 50+ edge locations; no server; static HTML/CSS/JS bundle
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions workflow: on PR - run Lighthouse CI against Netlify preview URL, fail if any CWV score <90; on merge to main - Netlify auto-deploy triggers; Netlify scheduled build every 6 hours to refresh stats data
devops.environments: Netlify branch previews (automatic per PR with unique URL), production (main branch), no staging environment needed for static page

backend.service_design: No backend service; Astro build script (src/data/fetchRepoStats.ts) acts as build-time data fetcher calling repository stats API
backend.api_patterns: Single GET /api/public/stats call returning {dataset_stats, recent_depositions, usage_metrics} payload; response cached in .astro/data/ during build to avoid redundant calls within single build

target_output.thinking_summary: Landing page for a scientific data repository with no backend, no database, no authentication. Architecture centers on Astro build-time data fetching from the repository stats API, React islands for D3 visualizations, and Netlify CDN delivery. Key decisions: client:visible hydration to hit >90 CWV, tree-shaken D3 imports to control island bundle size, Plausible Analytics with GDPR consent gate for privacy-preserving visitor tracking, scheduled Netlify rebuilds every 6 hours to keep statistics fresh without any real-time complexity.
target_output.title: Scientific Data Repository Landing Page Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A statically generated Astro 4 landing page with React islands for interactive D3 visualizations, deployed to Netlify CDN, showcasing the scientific data repository's dataset volume by domain, recent depositions, and usage metrics to convert visiting researchers into registered depositors. All repository statistics are fetched from the public stats API at Astro build time and embedded into static HTML, eliminating runtime backend dependency and maximizing Core Web Vitals scores. Plausible Analytics provides GDPR-compliant visitor behavior tracking with a cookie consent gate.
target_output.architecture_overview: Astro 4 static site with zero-JS HTML for non-interactive sections and React islands hydrated client:visible for D3 charts and the sign-up CTA component. Build pipeline runs a TypeScript data fetcher (src/data/fetchRepoStats.ts) that calls the repository public stats API once per build, serializing {dataset_stats, recent_depositions, usage_metrics} into static data passed to Astro page components. Netlify builds and deploys the site automatically on main branch push and on a 6-hour cron schedule to refresh statistics. No runtime server, no database. CSP headers served via netlify.toml block all inline scripts; Plausible Analytics script injected only after explicit GDPR consent stored in localStorage.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Astro 4.x with TypeScript, React 18 for hydration islands, Tailwind CSS v3 for utility styling, D3.js v7 (tree-shaken: selection, scale, arc, pie, format modules), framer-motion for animated stat counters. Analytics: Plausible Analytics (self-hosted script on repository subdomain for GDPR). Build tooling: Astro built-in Vite bundler, TypeScript strict mode. Hosting: Netlify CDN with edge delivery. CI: GitHub Actions with Lighthouse CI package. No backend runtime, no database, no auth library.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Hero Section: static Astro component displaying animated total dataset count (integer counter island hydrating on page load) and tagline with primary CTA button. Domain Distribution Section: DomainDistributionChart React island (client:visible) rendering a D3 donut chart of dataset counts across Life Sciences, Earth Sciences, Physics, Social Sciences, and Chemistry domains with hover tooltips. Recent Depositions Feed: static Astro list of 10 most recent dataset depositions with title, domain badge, DOI link, deposition date, and file size, embedded from build-time data fetch. Usage Metrics Row: static count display for total downloads, citations, and active registered researchers. Researcher Sign-Up CTA: React island with email input posting to repository registration API with referral tracking query param from URL.
target_output.system_components: fetchRepoStats.ts (Astro build-time script): calls GET /api/public/stats on repository platform, validates response schema with Zod, writes parsed data to src/data/repoStats.json consumed by page components. DomainDistributionChart island: D3 donut chart with ordinal color scale mapped to domain taxonomy, client:visible hydration, resize observer for responsive reflow. AnimatedStatCounter island: uses framer-motion useMotionValue to count up to target integer on client:load, displaying total datasets. ConsentBanner island: GDPR-compliant cookie consent with accept/decline; on accept injects Plausible script tag and sets consent=granted in localStorage. ResearcherCTA island: controlled email input, POST to repository /api/register with referral_source=landing_page query param, success state shows confirmation message.
target_output.workflows: Build-Time Stats Refresh: GitHub Actions or Netlify scheduled build trigger executes astro build; fetchRepoStats.ts runs, calls repository stats API GET /api/public/stats, validates {total_datasets: int, by_domain: [{name, count}], recent_depositions: [{title, doi, domain, deposited_at, size_gb}], downloads_total: int, citations_total: int, active_researchers: int}, writes to src/data/repoStats.json; Astro page components import JSON and embed values as static HTML or pass as island props; build completes in <90 seconds. Researcher Sign-Up Flow: visitor arrives on landing page, scrolls through domain chart and depositions, clicks 'Sign Up Free' CTA; ResearcherCTA island renders email input; on submit POSTs to repository /api/register with email and referral_source=landing_page; on 202 response, island shows 'Check your inbox' confirmation; Plausible custom event cta_click fires if consent granted.
target_output.data_model: All data is build-time static, not persisted. dataset_stats: total_count INT, by_domain Array<{domain_name STRING, count INT, color_hex STRING}>, updated_at ISO8601 STRING. recent_depositions: Array<{title STRING, domain STRING, doi STRING, deposited_at DATE, size_gb FLOAT}> - 10 most recent entries. usage_metrics: total_downloads INT, total_citations INT, active_researchers INT. These structures are defined as TypeScript interfaces in src/types/repoStats.ts and validated at build time with Zod schemas; build fails if API response does not conform, preventing broken statistics from deploying.
target_output.api_design: No server-side API exposed by this site. Consumed APIs: GET https://repository-platform.org/api/public/stats (external, called at build time only, returns application/json). POST https://repository-platform.org/api/register (external, called from ResearcherCTA island on form submission, accepts {email: string}, returns 202 Accepted). Plausible custom event POST to https://plausible.io/api/event for cta_click and domain_filter_interaction events fired client-side after consent.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Content Security Policy via netlify.toml _headers: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://plausible.io; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' data: https:; connect-src 'self' https://repository-platform.org https://plausible.io. X-Frame-Options: DENY. X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff. Referrer-Policy: strict-origin. HTTPS enforced by Netlify (HSTS header). GDPR: Plausible Analytics loaded only after ConsentBanner explicit accept; consent persisted in localStorage; no cookie-based tracking, no third-party trackers. No server-side secrets in repository. Repository stats API key stored in Netlify environment variable REPO_STATS_API_KEY, accessed only in build context never exposed to browser.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Netlify site connected to GitHub repository main branch; merge to main triggers automatic deploy; pull requests generate branch preview URLs with unique subdomains for visual review. Scheduled rebuild: Netlify build hook triggered by GitHub Actions cron workflow at 00:00/06:00/12:00/18:00 UTC to refresh repository statistics in built HTML. Build configuration in netlify.toml: base build command 'npm run build', publish dir 'dist', node version pinned to 20.x. Deploy notifications: Netlify Slack integration posts deploy status to #web-deploys channel. Rollback: Netlify one-click rollback to any prior deploy via dashboard.
target_output.observability: Plausible Analytics dashboard: page views by day, traffic sources, CTA click conversion rate, domain chart interaction rate, geographic distribution. GitHub Actions Lighthouse CI workflow: runs on each PR against Netlify preview URL, enforces minimum scores Performance >90, Accessibility >95, Best Practices >90, SEO >90; PR blocked if thresholds missed. Netlify build duration tracked in build logs; alert set up via Netlify notification webhook if build time exceeds 3 minutes. No application-level error monitoring needed for static page; 4xx/5xx tracked by Netlify analytics.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Netlify Starter plan: $0/month for static hosting (within free bandwidth limits). Estimated monthly traffic for academic marketing page: ~5,000 page views, well within Netlify free tier 100GB bandwidth. Plausible Analytics: $9/month (10K pageviews plan). Total: ~$9/month. Scaling: Netlify CDN auto-scales globally with no configuration; no compute to scale. If traffic exceeds 100GB/month bandwidth, upgrade to Netlify Pro ($19/month) provides 1TB. D3 island bundle size target: <40KB gzipped for domain chart to maintain <2s LCP on 4G connection.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (3 weeks): static Astro page with embedded build-time repository stats, DomainDistributionChart D3 island, RecentDepositions feed, AnimatedStatCounter, ResearcherCTA with repository registration POST, GDPR ConsentBanner, CSP headers, Lighthouse CI gate, Netlify deployment and 6-hour scheduled rebuild. Phase 2 (post-launch): headless CMS integration (Contentful or Sanity) for marketing team self-service text and imagery updates without code deploys; CTA copy A/B testing via Netlify split testing feature. Phase 3: personalized domain-interest routing based on URL query param from researcher emails driving return visits to domain-specific content sections.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode enforced across all Astro and React island files. Tailwind classes only via className, no inline styles. D3 imports strictly named (no import * as d3) to ensure tree-shaking. fetchRepoStats.ts validated with Zod at build time - build fails loudly on schema mismatch rather than deploying stale or broken stats. Lighthouse CI score thresholds enforced in PR checks. No analytics calls in non-island Astro components to prevent server-side invocations. All Netlify environment variables documented in .env.example with placeholder values.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Repository stats API downtime at build time fails the Netlify build and pauses stats refresh; mitigation: fall back to cached src/data/repoStats.json from last successful build using try/catch in fetchRepoStats.ts, logging a build warning. Risk: D3 chart island increases JS bundle beyond CWV threshold; mitigation: Lighthouse CI threshold gate catches regressions in PR before merge. Tradeoff: build-time data over client-side fetch accepted for security (API key not exposed to browser) and CWV (no waterfall on chart load) despite 6-hour data lag. Tradeoff: Astro over Next.js chosen for true zero-JS static output without needing careful SSG configuration, at cost of smaller ecosystem.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Repository stats API confirmed to return pre-aggregated JSON suitable for single build-time call. Plausible Analytics selected over GA4 to avoid GDPR consent complexity for EU researchers. Netlify chosen over Vercel for simpler scheduled build hook mechanism. D3 confirmed over Chart.js for design flexibility in donut chart domain visualization.

metadata.notes: Static Astro landing page for scientific data repository. Build-time stats fetch, D3 islands, Plausible analytics, Netlify CDN.

project_goal.value: Real-time environmental monitoring web application for cleanroom facilities in semiconductor research fabs, displaying live particle counter, temperature, and humidity sensor readings with automated alarm escalation for out-of-specification conditions
project_goal.rationale: Core operational need for ISO 14644 cleanroom compliance monitoring

target_users.value: Cleanroom facility engineers, process technicians, and EHS supervisors
target_users.rationale: Primary operators responsible for cleanroom environmental compliance

project_class.rationale: Browser-based dashboard with real-time sensor data, no heavy client-side installation required

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, realtime, analytics
capabilities.rationale: Requires live WebSocket feeds, time-series storage, RBAC auth, and trend analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Real-time MQTT ingestion and WebSocket delivery add complexity but domain is well-defined

risk_level.rationale: Missed alarms could result in process contamination; monitoring reliability is critical

data_sensitivity.rationale: Sensor telemetry and cleanroom specs are proprietary but not customer or health data

external_exposure.rationale: System operates on isolated facility network with no external internet access

access_model.value: RBAC with Keycloak: technician read-only, engineer full access, supervisor alarm management
access_model.rationale: Different roles require different access to alarm configuration and sensor calibration data

feature_scope.value: Live sensor dashboard with zone-level views, alarm rule configuration, alarm escalation workflows, historical trend charts, shift compliance report export
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for ISO 14644 continuous monitoring requirements

mvp_scope.value: Live particle counter and temperature readings for 3 cleanroom zones, basic threshold alarms with email notifications, 30-day historical trends
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for initial ISO 14644 audit readiness

security_baseline.value: Keycloak SSO with internal LDAP federation, TLS 1.3 on internal network, role-based access control, audit log for alarm acknowledgements
security_baseline.rationale: Facility security policy requires centralized SSO and access logging

frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise and rich ecosystem for real-time dashboard components

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Fiber
backend_stack.rationale: Go provides low-latency MQTT message processing and efficient WebSocket handling

data_platform.value: InfluxDB 2.7 for time-series sensor data, Redis 7 for real-time state and alarm caching
data_platform.rationale: InfluxDB optimized for high-frequency sensor ingestion and time-range queries

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal Kubernetes on facility network
hosting_target.rationale: Air-gapped facility network prohibits cloud hosting; on-prem Kubernetes provides orchestration

privacy_retention_policy.value: Sensor readings retained 2 years for ISO 14644 compliance records, alarm events retained 5 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: ISO 14644-2 requires 2-year minimum data retention for cleanroom monitoring records

future_scope.value: Predictive alarm analytics, mobile app for on-call engineers, integration with wafer defect tracking system
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap contingent on successful MVP deployment

constraints.value: Must operate fully offline on isolated facility network, sensor hardware uses MQTT 3.1.1 protocol, existing Keycloak 22 SSO must be reused
constraints.rationale: Facility network isolation and existing infrastructure constraints

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for infrastructure metrics, structured JSON logging, alarm delivery success rate tracked
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing Grafana stack already deployed in facility

execution_preference.value: Iterative 2-week sprints with early sensor integration to validate MQTT pipeline
execution_preference.rationale: De-risk real-time data flow early given hardware dependency

project.goal: Monitor cleanroom environmental parameters in real time and escalate alarms for out-of-spec conditions
project.target_users: Cleanroom engineers, technicians, EHS supervisors

frontend.charting: Recharts for real-time trend sparklines and historical charts
frontend.state: Zustand for alarm state, React Query for historical data

backend.mqtt_client: Eclipse Paho Go client subscribing to sensor topics

security.auth: Keycloak OIDC with LDAP federation

data.cache: Redis 7 for latest readings per sensor node and active alarm state

devops.environments: staging (lab bench), production (cleanroom network)

open_questions.alarm_channels: Whether SMS gateway is available in addition to email for alarm escalation

reasoner_reviews.product: The scope is appropriate for cleanroom environmental monitoring. Live particle counts, temperature, and humidity are the three critical parameters for ISO 14644-2. The alarm escalation workflow is essential because missed out-of-spec events can result in wafer batch contamination and process holds.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go with Fiber is a solid choice for MQTT-to-WebSocket fanout at this sensor density. MQTT subscriber goroutines can write latest readings to Redis and fan out to connected WebSocket clients with minimal latency. InfluxDB Flux queries handle time-range aggregations natively. Tag InfluxDB measurements by zone_id and sensor_node_id for efficient filtering without full scans.
reasoner_reviews.security: Internal-only deployment reduces external attack surface significantly. Keycloak OIDC integration is appropriate. Alarm acknowledgement actions must be audit-logged with user identity and timestamp for ISO 14644-2 traceability requirements. TLS 1.3 should be enforced even on internal network segments.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Air-gapped deployment means all container images must be pre-pulled to on-premises Harbor registry. GitLab runner and Helm charts must be validated against the facility Kubernetes version. MQTT broker must support sensor hardware TLS client certificate configuration if required by vendor.
