Database migrations via Sequelize CLI run as pre-deploy step in GitHub Actions before new container goes live.
target_output.observability: New Relic APM agent in Express application tracking transaction traces with experiment_id and user_id custom attributes. Structured JSON logs (experiment_created, experiment_countersigned, file_scan_result, auth_failure) shipped to New Relic Logs. Custom metrics: experiments_countersigned_per_day, file_uploads_per_day, file_quarantine_rate. New Relic Alerts: API error rate > 2%, p95 latency > 3s, ClamAV scan queue depth > 20.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $380/month: DigitalOcean App Platform Basic $50, managed PostgreSQL $50, managed Redis $20, Spaces storage $50 (2TB), Spaces CDN bandwidth $30, ClamAV compute included in App Platform. Under $500 budget target. Scales via App Platform auto-scaling (1-3 instances) based on CPU threshold. Spaces object storage scales linearly with attachment volume.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): experiment record creation with Tiptap structured sections, experiment_versions append-only table, PI countersigning with record locking, file attachment via Spaces presigned URLs with ClamAV scan, Auth0 SAML SSO, lab membership with RBAC, GitHub Actions CI/CD, New Relic APM. Phase 2 (4 weeks): protocol library with versioning, protocol-to-experiment linking, version diff view, collaborator role for external lab sharing, PI review queue dashboard. Phase 3 (4 weeks): usage analytics dashboard, bulk experiment export to PDF, instrument API integration stubs, email notification system.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode. ESLint with eslint-plugin-security for common injection patterns. 70% unit test coverage enforced in CI (Jest). Experiment version saves must be atomic: create experiment_version row and update experiment_records.current_version_id in a single PostgreSQL transaction. File upload confirm endpoint must validate that Spaces quarantine object exists before triggering ClamAV scan. Auth0 JWT validation middleware must be applied to all /api/v1 routes with no exceptions.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: 2GB file uploads from slow university network connections time out or fail mid-transfer. Mitigation: DigitalOcean Spaces supports multipart upload resumption; implement client-side upload retry with exponential backoff. Risk: ClamAV definitions become stale on long-running App Platform instances. Mitigation: scheduled daily definition update job; alert if definitions older than 48 hours. Tradeoff: monolithic Express application for MVP simplicity over microservices; experiment and file modules have clear boundaries for future extraction if needed. Tradeoff: Tiptap structured sections use fixed field names (hypothesis, materials, procedure, observations) rather than configurable templates, which may not suit all research disciplines in the pilot group.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected per-save versioning over per-section-submit; researchers save frequently and granular history is valuable for GLP audits. Selected DigitalOcean over AWS S3 for Spaces to stay within single cloud provider and budget. Auth0 chosen over in-house SAML handling for institutional SSO to reduce engineering complexity. ClamAV over commercial AV for cost reasons given academic budget.

metadata.notes: Weak: observability and cost sections at minimum detail; development_guidelines are functional but not deeply domain-specific; system is well-structured but several sections lack the depth of a strong plan.

project_goal.value: AI-powered scientific literature triage system for research teams, using language models to automatically screen incoming PubMed abstracts against user-defined research criteria, score relevance, extract key findings, and route papers to appropriate team members
project_goal.rationale: Research teams spend 4-6 hours weekly manually screening PubMed alerts; automation reduces overhead

target_users.value: Academic research team members, principal investigators, and literature review coordinators
target_users.rationale: Primary users managing incoming paper feeds and review assignments

project_class.rationale: Core value is LLM-based relevance scoring and finding extraction from scientific abstracts

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, ai_llm, integrations, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: React dashboard, FastAPI backend, LLM scoring workers, PubMed API integration, daily batch ingestion jobs

complexity_level.rationale: LLM scoring pipeline, feedback loop retraining, microservices decomposition add significant complexity

risk_level.rationale: Incorrect triage recommendation has no safety consequence; researcher reviews before acting on suggestions

data_sensitivity.rationale: Team research criteria and screening decisions are proprietary but not regulated data

external_exposure.rationale: Login required for all access; no public-facing endpoints

access_model.value: Cognito-based RBAC: team member (review feed), PI (manage criteria and assignments), admin (system config)
access_model.rationale: Research teams need role separation between consumers and managers of screening criteria

feature_scope.value: PubMed subscription management, daily abstract ingestion, LLM relevance scoring with supporting quote extraction, personalized paper feed per team member, include/exclude review decisions, feedback loop for score improvement, assignment routing rules
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for replacing manual PubMed alert screening workflow

mvp_scope.rationale: Single-team MVP validates LLM scoring quality before multi-team rollout

security_baseline.value: Cognito authentication with JWT, HTTPS only, API keys for PubMed E-utilities in Secrets Manager, rate limiting on API endpoints
security_baseline.rationale: Standard security baseline for internal research tooling on AWS

frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; rich ecosystem for feed-style UI components

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI
backend_stack.rationale: Python native LLM SDK integrations and FastAPI async performance

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for paper metadata and review decisions, Redis 7 for job queue and scoring task cache
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL full-text search for paper queries; Redis Queues for LLM scoring job dispatch

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers with auto-scaling for variable LLM scoring worker demand

privacy_retention_policy.value: PubMed abstract text and metadata retained indefinitely as public data; user review decisions retained 3 years; scoring model feedback data retained 2 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Model feedback data has defined utility horizon; review decisions needed for audit trail

future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 features after MVP validation

constraints.rationale: Budget constraint drives LLM provider and batching strategy selection

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for logs and metrics, LLM scoring latency and cost per abstract tracked, daily ingestion job success/failure alerted
observability_baseline.rationale: CloudWatch available on AWS without additional tooling cost

execution_preference.value: Agile 2-week sprints, LLM scoring quality validated by team before advancing to full feed UI
execution_preference.rationale: De-risk LLM scoring accuracy early before investing in feed UI

llm_integration.value: OpenAI GPT-4o-mini for relevance scoring (cost-optimized); structured output mode for JSON relevance score and supporting quote extraction; system prompt encodes team research criteria
llm_integration.rationale: GPT-4o-mini provides sufficient abstract understanding at $0.15/1M input tokens; structured output ensures parseable JSON responses

project.goal: Automate PubMed abstract screening with LLM relevance scoring and personalized team feeds
project.target_users: Research team members and PIs

frontend.state: React Query for paper feed, Zustand for review decision state
frontend.ui: TailwindCSS, abstract card components with score badges

backend.workers: ARQ Redis queue workers for LLM scoring jobs

security.auth: Cognito user pools with JWT
security.secrets: AWS Secrets Manager for OpenAI and PubMed API keys

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector extension for future embedding support
data.queue: Redis 7 via ARQ for scoring job dispatch

devops.hosting: ECS Fargate with auto-scaling on queue depth
devops.environments: dev, staging, production

open_questions.feedback_loop: Whether to use fine-tuning or in-context few-shot examples from feedback data for score improvement

confirmed_decisions.queue: ARQ over Celery for simpler async/await integration

reasoner_reviews.product: The core loop of daily PubMed ingestion, LLM scoring, and personalized feed is well-scoped for MVP. Restricting MVP to a single team with 3 configurable criteria is pragmatic for validating LLM scoring quality before scaling. The include/exclude feedback loop is the most important feature for improving relevance over time.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Microservices decomposition into ingestion-service, scoring-service, and feed-service is appropriate for independent scaling of LLM worker capacity. However, for MVP a modular monolith may be faster to ship. ARQ Redis queue is a good choice over Celery for clean async/await integration with FastAPI. GPT-4o-mini structured output with a fixed JSON schema for relevance_score, supporting_quote, and suggested_assignee will be reliable.
reasoner_reviews.security: OpenAI API key must be in Secrets Manager, never in environment variables directly. Cognito JWT validation should happen at ALB layer to prevent unauthenticated requests reaching the FastAPI service. Abstract content from PubMed is public, but team criteria prompts and review decisions must not be logged in plaintext to CloudWatch.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 500 abstracts/day at average 300 tokens per abstract input plus 100 tokens output at $0.15/1M input and $0.60/1M output tokens = approximately $25/month, well under the $200 budget. This leaves headroom for score quality improvement prompts. PubMed E-utilities batch fetch of up to 200 records per request should be used to stay within rate limits.

backend.service_design: Three logical modules in FastAPI monolith: ingestion (PubMed E-utilities fetch, deduplication), scoring (OpenAI GPT-4o-mini structured output, ARQ job dispatch), feed_api (paper retrieval, review decision endpoints)
backend.llm_integration_design: System prompt template with team criteria injected at job creation time; user message contains title and abstract text; response_format=json_schema enforcing {relevance_score: 0-10, supporting_quote: str, suggested_assignee: str|null, rationale: str}; batch up to 20 abstracts in parallel ARQ jobs to maximize throughput within ECS Fargate worker count
backend.background_jobs: Daily PubMed ingestion ARQ job triggered at 06:00 UTC; fetches new PMIDs matching team MeSH subscriptions via esearch, batch-fetches efetch records, deduplicates against literature_items table, enqueues scoring jobs for new items
backend.failure_handling: OpenAI API timeout retried 3 times with exponential backoff; failed scoring jobs moved to dead_letter queue after 3 retries; PubMed fetch failures logged with CloudWatch alert; partial ingestion resumable via last_fetched_pmid checkpoint

frontend.app_structure: React SPA: PaperFeed, PaperDetail, CriteriaManager, AssignmentRules, TeamDashboard, AdminConfig
frontend.pages_and_flows: PaperFeed shows infinite-scroll cards with abstract, relevance_score badge color-coded by threshold, supporting_quote highlight, and include/exclude action buttons; CriteriaManager allows PIs to add/edit/delete screening criteria that update the scoring prompt
frontend.state_management: React Query for paginated paper feed with optimistic mutations for include/exclude decisions; Zustand for criteria editor draft state

security.auth_design: Cognito user pool with Hosted UI; ALB authenticates requests via Cognito action before forwarding to FastAPI; FastAPI reads JWT claims for user_id and role
security.authorization_model: Team member: read feed and submit review decisions; PI: manage criteria and assignment rules; Admin: system configuration
security.secrets_management: OpenAI API key and NCBI API key in Secrets Manager, fetched at container startup via boto3; never written to logs

data.entities: literature_items (pmid, title, abstract_text, authors_json, journal, publication_date, mesh_terms_json, ingested_at), screening_criteria (id, team_id FK, criterion_text, weight, active), relevance_scores (id, item_id FK, criteria_id FK, score INT 0-10, supporting_quote, rationale, model_version, scored_at), team_assignments (id, item_id FK, assigned_to UUID FK, assignment_reason, routed_at), review_decisions (id, item_id FK, reviewer_id FK, decision ENUM include/exclude, notes, decided_at), topic_subscriptions (id, team_id FK, mesh_term, active)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 for all relational data; pgvector extension reserved for Phase 2 embedding similarity search; Redis 7 via ARQ for scoring job queue and result caching

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate: api-service (2 tasks, ALB target), scoring-worker (0-5 tasks, auto-scales on ARQ queue depth CloudWatch metric), ingestion-worker (1 task, cron scheduled), Redis ElastiCache, RDS PostgreSQL
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest with 80% coverage gate, mypy type checking, Docker build and push to ECR, ECS service update rolling deployment
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch Logs with structured JSON, CloudWatch Metrics for LLM scoring latency and cost per abstract, daily ingestion job success/failure alarm

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a scientific literature triage AI system on ECS Fargate with OpenAI GPT-4o-mini. Key decisions: ARQ Redis queue for async LLM scoring job dispatch, modular FastAPI monolith for MVP speed over premature microservices split, structured JSON output schema for reliable relevance score and quote extraction. Plan quality intentionally weak - several sections at minimum detail level to reflect weak plan quality distribution requirement.
target_output.title: Scientific Literature Triage AI Platform
target_output.executive_summary: An AI-powered literature screening system for academic research teams built with React 18 and FastAPI on ECS Fargate. Daily batch jobs fetch new PubMed abstracts matching team MeSH subscriptions, ARQ Redis queue workers submit abstracts to GPT-4o-mini with structured output for relevance scoring against team criteria, and researchers review personalized feeds of pre-scored papers with include/exclude decisions feeding a continuous improvement loop.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier web application with React 18 SPA behind CloudFront communicating with FastAPI REST API on ECS Fargate via ALB with Cognito authentication. Daily scheduled ECS task fetches new PubMed abstracts via NCBI E-utilities, deduplicates against PostgreSQL, and enqueues scoring jobs to Redis ARQ queue. ECS scoring workers consume jobs, call OpenAI GPT-4o-mini with structured JSON output schema, and persist relevance scores and supporting quotes. Team members access personalized paper feeds filtered by relevance score threshold and assigned by topic subscription. CloudWatch tracks LLM API cost and ingestion job health.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, TailwindCSS, React Query, Zustand. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, ARQ for Redis-backed async job queue, openai SDK with structured output. Database: PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector extension. Cache/Queue: Redis 7 via ElastiCache. Auth: AWS Cognito user pools with ALB integration. LLM: OpenAI GPT-4o-mini with JSON schema response_format. Infra: ECS Fargate, ALB, RDS PostgreSQL, ElastiCache Redis, CloudFront, Secrets Manager, GitHub Actions, ECR.
target_output.functional_feature_map: PubMed Subscription Management: team PI configures MeSH term subscriptions that drive daily esearch queries. Daily Ingestion Job: scheduled ECS task fetches new PMIDs, batch-retrieves efetch records for deduplication and storage. LLM Scoring Pipeline: ARQ workers score each new abstract against team criteria using GPT-4o-mini structured output. Paper Feed UI: infinite-scroll cards with relevance badge, supporting quote highlight, and include/exclude buttons. Criteria Manager: PI creates and weights screening criteria that are injected into scoring prompt template. Review Decision Storage: include/exclude decisions with notes stored for feedback loop. Assignment Routing: rule-based routing of papers to team members by topic subscription.
target_output.system_components: CloudFront distribution for React SPA static assets. ALB with Cognito authentication action forwarding authenticated requests to FastAPI ECS service. ECS Fargate api-service (2 tasks) for REST endpoints. ECS Fargate scoring-worker (0-5 tasks, auto-scales on ARQ queue depth). ECS Fargate ingestion-worker (1 task, cron-scheduled). RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ for literature_items and review decisions. ElastiCache Redis 7 for ARQ job queue and scoring results. Secrets Manager for OpenAI and NCBI API keys. CloudWatch for logs, metrics, and alarms.
target_output.workflows: Daily Ingestion Workflow: CloudWatch Events triggers ingestion-worker ECS task at 06:00 UTC; task calls NCBI esearch with team MeSH terms to retrieve new PMIDs since last_fetched_date checkpoint; batch-calls efetch for up to 200 PMIDs per request; filters PMIDs already in literature_items; inserts new records with abstract text and mesh_terms_json; enqueues one ARQ scoring job per new item. LLM Scoring Workflow: ARQ worker dequeues scoring job with item_id; fetches literature_item abstract and team screening_criteria; constructs GPT-4o-mini prompt with system message containing criteria and user message with title and abstract; calls OpenAI API with response_format JSON schema {relevance_score, supporting_quote, suggested_assignee, rationale}; stores result in relevance_scores table; paper becomes visible in team member feeds once scored.
target_output.data_model: literature_items (pmid BIGINT PK, title TEXT, abstract_text TEXT, authors_json JSONB, journal TEXT, publication_date DATE, mesh_terms_json JSONB, ingested_at TIMESTAMPTZ). screening_criteria (id UUID PK, team_id UUID FK, criterion_text TEXT, weight FLOAT, active BOOL). relevance_scores (id UUID PK, item_id BIGINT FK, score INT CHECK 0-10, supporting_quote TEXT, rationale TEXT, model_version TEXT, scored_at TIMESTAMPTZ). review_decisions (id UUID PK, item_id BIGINT FK, reviewer_id UUID FK, decision ENUM include/exclude, notes TEXT, decided_at TIMESTAMPTZ). topic_subscriptions (id UUID PK, team_id UUID FK, mesh_term TEXT, active BOOL). team_assignments (id UUID PK, item_id BIGINT FK, assigned_to UUID FK, routed_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: REST API at /api/v1 with Cognito JWT auth. GET /feed?page=&min_score= returns paginated relevance_scores with paper metadata for authenticated user. POST /decisions creates include/exclude review_decision for a paper. GET /criteria returns team screening_criteria list. POST /criteria creates new screening criterion. PUT /criteria/{id} updates criterion text or weight. GET /subscriptions returns MeSH term subscriptions. POST /subscriptions adds MeSH term subscription. GET /stats returns team feed summary with decision rate and average score.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cognito user pool with Hosted UI for team member authentication; ALB Cognito auth action blocks unauthenticated requests before FastAPI. JWT claims carry user_id and role; FastAPI dependency extracts role for endpoint authorization. OpenAI and NCBI API keys stored in Secrets Manager, fetched at startup by boto3 into in-memory config; never logged. Abstract text sent to OpenAI is PubMed public data; team criteria prompts are internal and should not be logged in CloudWatch in plaintext. HTTPS enforced on all endpoints.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: pytest 80% coverage gate, mypy strict type checking, Docker multi-stage build, push to ECR, ECS rolling update. ECS service definitions: api-service fixed at 2 tasks with ALB health check; scoring-worker with Step Scaling policy adding tasks when ARQ queue depth exceeds 50 unprocessed jobs, scaling in when queue drains; ingestion-worker as scheduled ECS task triggered by EventBridge daily cron. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ with 7-day automated backups. Secrets Manager rotation enabled for database credentials with 30-day interval.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch Logs with structured JSON from FastAPI and ARQ workers. Custom CloudWatch Metrics: llm_cost_per_abstract (OpenAI token count * price), scoring_queue_depth (ARQ pending jobs), ingestion_new_items_count (daily), review_decision_rate (decisions/day). CloudWatch Alarms: ingestion job failure (zero new items ingested when expected), scoring queue depth > 200 for > 30 minutes (worker scaling insufficient), OpenAI API error rate > 5%.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: LLM cost: 500 abstracts/day at ~300 input tokens + 100 output tokens = 150k input + 50k output tokens/day at $0.15/$0.60 per 1M tokens = approximately $25/month, under $200 budget. ECS Fargate: api-service 2x 0.5vCPU/1GB ~$30/month; scoring workers scale to zero when queue empty. RDS db.t3.medium ~$50/month. Total estimated $120/month.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): PubMed ingestion for one team, LLM scoring pipeline with GPT-4o-mini, basic paper feed UI with include/exclude decisions, Cognito auth, CI/CD pipeline. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Multi-team support, criteria manager UI for PIs, assignment routing rules, scoring quality dashboard showing decision correlation with LLM scores. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Feedback-loop few-shot example injection from high-confidence review decisions into scoring prompts, Zotero export integration, pgvector embedding similarity search for related paper recommendations.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python: mypy strict mode, pytest with 80% coverage, ruff linter. TypeScript strict mode, ESLint. LLM prompts version-controlled as Jinja2 template files with changelog. OpenAI API calls mocked in tests using responses library. No hardcoded API keys anywhere in codebase.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GPT-4o-mini relevance scoring quality insufficient for research team adoption. Mitigation: score calibration sprint in Phase 1 where team manually validates 100 scored abstracts against their own judgment before feed UI launch. Risk: OpenAI structured output JSON schema changes break parsing. Mitigation: Pydantic model validation on scoring response with fallback to score=None for malformed output. Tradeoff: modular monolith over microservices for MVP speed at cost of less independent scaling granularity for scoring workers.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected GPT-4o-mini over GPT-4o for cost constraint compliance. ARQ over Celery for cleaner async/await integration with FastAPI. PostgreSQL over DynamoDB for complex query needs on paper feed filtering. Feedback loop approach deferred to Phase 3 - few-shot injection from review decisions preferred over fine-tuning for operational simplicity.

metadata.notes: AI literature triage system with GPT-4o-mini PubMed screening; weak quality - several thin sections, microservices decomposition discussed but deferred.

project_goal.value: High-scale genomics API service exposing variant annotation endpoints for clinical and research sequencing pipelines, accepting VCF-format variant calls and returning population frequency data, pathogenicity predictions, and literature evidence annotations for millions of variants
project_goal.rationale: Core mission is providing authoritative variant interpretation data to clinical labs and research pipelines via a partner-facing API

target_users.value: Clinical sequencing laboratories, genomics research organizations, and bioinformatics pipeline developers integrating variant interpretation into their workflows
target_users.rationale: Primary API consumers are programmatic integrations in production clinical and research workflows

project_class.rationale: Pure API backend with no browser frontend; FrontendAgent not applicable

capabilities.value: backend, data, auth, public_api, batch_jobs, analytics
capabilities.rationale: High-throughput variant lookup, scoped API key auth, bulk annotation jobs, and usage analytics required

complexity_level.rationale: Hundreds of millions of variants in reference databases; sub-second lookup SLA for real-time annotation; asynchronous bulk jobs for large VCF files

risk_level.rationale: Clinical labs use annotation data for variant pathogenicity calls that directly inform patient diagnoses; incorrect data or unauthorized access constitutes patient safety risk

data_sensitivity.rationale: When processing patient-linked VCF files, variant data constitutes PHI under HIPAA

external_exposure.rationale: API accessed by partner clinical labs and research organizations via scoped API keys; not public internet

access_model.value: Scoped API keys with permissions: annotate_realtime (single variant and small batch), annotate_bulk (large VCF jobs), admin (key management); rate limits per key tier
access_model.rationale: Partner access control with differentiated rate limits based on SLA tier

feature_scope.value: Single variant annotation (real-time, sub-second), small batch VCF annotation (up to 10K variants, synchronous), bulk VCF job submission (millions of variants, async), population frequency lookup against gnomAD v4 and ClinVar, pathogenicity prediction (CADD, SIFT, PolyPhen-2 scores), literature evidence citations, annotation versioning for reproducibility
feature_scope.rationale: Full variant interpretation data required for clinical decision support integration

mvp_scope.value: Single variant and small batch synchronous annotation with gnomAD v4 population frequencies and ClinVar pathogenicity classifications; API key authentication with rate limiting
mvp_scope.rationale: Real-time annotation is the highest-priority use case for clinical lab partner integrations

security_baseline.value: mTLS for partner API connections, API keys hashed with Argon2 before storage, TLS 1.3, all API request and response payloads containing patient-linked variants logged with HIPAA-compliant PHI handling
security_baseline.rationale: HIPAA and partner security requirements for clinical data API

frontend_stack.value: No frontend; API-only service with OpenAPI 3.1 documentation hosted at /docs
frontend_stack.rationale: FrontendAgent not applicable for pure API service

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI; Celery workers for bulk annotation jobs
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI async performance for high-throughput annotation; Celery for large VCF job processing

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for variant annotation lookups (columnar storage, sub-second aggregation on 500M+ rows); PostgreSQL 16 for API keys, job metadata, and billing; Redis 7 for rate limiting and job status
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse columnar storage is required for sub-second population frequency queries across hundreds of millions of variant records

hosting_target.value: GCP GKE Autopilot in us-central1
hosting_target.rationale: GKE Autopilot reduces node management overhead; GCP chosen for BigQuery integration with public genomics datasets

privacy_retention_policy.value: Patient-linked variant query logs retained 6 years per HIPAA; non-PHI query logs retained 1 year; bulk job input VCF files deleted 72 hours after job completion
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: HIPAA minimum retention for PHI access logs; VCF file deletion limits PHI storage duration

future_scope.value: Somatic variant annotation, structural variant support, pharmacogenomics annotations, custom annotation track uploads, variant reporting API for clinical report generation
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP clinical genomics annotation expansion roadmap

constraints.value: Sub-second p99 latency for single variant annotation; VCF parsing must handle GRCh37 and GRCh38 reference genomes; no patient identifiers logged in application logs
constraints.rationale: Clinical lab SLA requirements and HIPAA prohibition on PHI in operational logs

observability_baseline.value: Grafana plus Prometheus for metrics; annotation query latency histograms per endpoint; Celery task monitoring via Flower; Datadog for APM traces
observability_baseline.rationale: High-scale API requires detailed performance visibility for SLA compliance monitoring

execution_preference.value: Iterative releases with partner lab pilot testing after each milestone
execution_preference.rationale: Clinical partner integrations require validation testing before production adoption

compliance_context.value: HIPAA for patient-linked variant data, GDPR for variant data linked to EU individuals, NIH Genomic Data Sharing Policy for any use of NIH-funded reference datasets, partner BAA required before clinical VCF processing
compliance_context.rationale: Clinical genomics API processing patient-linked variants has federal regulatory obligations

project.goal: Sub-second variant annotation API for clinical sequencing labs with gnomAD and ClinVar data integration
project.target_users: Clinical labs, research sequencing pipelines, bioinformatics developers
project.timeline: MVP real-time annotation in 12 weeks; bulk jobs in 20 weeks

backend.framework: FastAPI with async handlers; Celery for bulk jobs
backend.api: REST with OpenAPI 3.1; versioned endpoints at /v1

security.auth: Scoped API keys hashed with Argon2; Redis rate limiting per key
security.transport: TLS 1.3; mTLS for clinical partner connections
security.phi_handling: No patient identifiers in application logs; VCF files deleted 72h after job completion

data.db: ClickHouse (variant annotation tables); PostgreSQL 16 (API keys, job metadata)
data.cache: Redis 7 for rate limiting counters and job status
data.reference_data: gnomAD v4 (750M variants), ClinVar (2M entries), CADD score precomputed tables

devops.environments: staging, production

open_questions.reference_genome_liftover: Should liftover be performed on ingest or at query time?

confirmed_decisions.reference_data: gnomAD v4 plus ClinVar plus CADD

reasoner_reviews.product: The core value proposition is clear: clinical labs need sub-second variant annotation to integrate into real-time sequencing pipelines. The differentiation from existing tools (Ensembl VEP, ANNOVAR) is the managed API service model with SLA guarantees and partner-specific rate limiting. Priority should be gnomAD population frequency plus ClinVar pathogenicity in MVP - CADD and PolyPhen-2 scores can follow in a subsequent release.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: ClickHouse is the correct database for this use case - 750 million gnomAD v4 variants require columnar storage for sub-second population frequency queries that join on chromosome, position, reference, and alternate allele. FastAPI async is appropriate for high-throughput annotation. The main risk is ClickHouse data loading time for the full gnomAD v4 dataset during initial setup and updates.
reasoner_reviews.security: HIPAA compliance for patient-linked VCF requires careful PHI segregation. The constraint that no patient identifiers appear in application logs is critical but requires careful implementation - FastAPI request logging must mask or omit body content for annotation endpoints. mTLS for clinical partner connections should use per-partner client certificates to enable per-partner revocation. Partner BAA must be executed before any clinical-grade VCF is accepted.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Sub-second p99 for single variant annotation with ClickHouse is achievable with proper indexing on (chrom, pos, ref, alt) but requires careful ClickHouse MergeTree primary key design. GRCh37 to GRCh38 liftover should be performed at annotation data load time, not at query time, to avoid per-request liftover computation overhead.

backend.service_design: Three FastAPI service modules: AnnotationService (single variant and batch real-time annotation against ClickHouse), JobService (bulk VCF job submission, status polling, result delivery), KeyService (API key creation, rotation, and rate limit configuration). Celery worker fleet for bulk VCF parsing and ClickHouse batch annotation.
backend.api_patterns: REST at /v1; POST /v1/variants/annotate (single or small batch, synchronous); POST /v1/jobs (bulk VCF submission, asynchronous); GET /v1/jobs/{job_id} (status polling); GET /v1/jobs/{job_id}/results (completed job results download URL); GET /v1/keys (key listing for admin); POST /v1/keys (key creation for admin)
backend.failure_handling: ClickHouse query timeout (500ms) returns cached result if available in Redis, else 503 with retry-after. Celery task failures trigger automatic retry (3x with exponential backoff); dead-letter queue for permanently failed jobs with admin notification.
backend.scaling_notes: ClickHouse cluster: 3-node setup with replication; FastAPI pods scale on RPS via GKE HPA; Celery workers scale on queue depth via KEDA

security.auth_design: API key authentication: clients send X-API-Key header; FastAPI dependency resolves key, checks Argon2 hash against PostgreSQL api_keys table (with Redis cache for hash lookups), validates permissions scope, increments rate limit counter in Redis.
security.authorization_model: annotate_realtime scope: POST /v1/variants/annotate; annotate_bulk scope: POST /v1/jobs; admin scope: /v1/keys endpoints. Scope enforced in FastAPI Depends() dependency injected into route handlers.
security.secrets_management: GCP Secret Manager for ClickHouse credentials, PostgreSQL DSN, and Celery broker URL; secrets mounted as environment variables via GKE Workload Identity and External Secrets Operator.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: HIPAA audit log: every annotation request logged with api_key_id (not key value), client IP, timestamp, variant count, and annotation_version; no patient identifiers or variant data in logs. Logs shipped to GCP Cloud Logging with 6-year retention bucket.

data.entities: genomic_variants (ClickHouse MergeTree table: chrom LowCardinality(String), pos UInt32, ref String, alt String, genome_build LowCardinality(String), gnomad_af Float64, gnomad_af_popmax Float64, gnomad_ac UInt32, gnomad_an UInt32, clinvar_significance LowCardinality(String), clinvar_review_stars UInt8, cadd_phred Float32, annotation_version String, ORDER BY (genome_build, chrom, pos, ref, alt)). api_keys (PostgreSQL: id UUID PK, key_hash VARCHAR, client_name VARCHAR, permissions JSONB, rate_limit_per_minute INT, active BOOLEAN, created_at, last_used_at). annotation_jobs (PostgreSQL: id UUID PK, api_key_id FK, status ENUM pending/running/completed/failed, input_vcf_gcs_path VARCHAR, result_gcs_path VARCHAR, variant_count INT, submitted_at, completed_at). api_key_id (not to be confused with api_keys id - ensure naming is consistent).
data.storage_design: ClickHouse 3-node cluster on GKE with persistent volumes (3TB NVMe per node for gnomAD v4 full dataset); PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL for operational data; Redis 7 on Cloud Memorystore for rate limiting and job status; GCS bucket for VCF input/output files (72-hour lifecycle policy for input files)
data.analytics_events: annotation_request_count (per api_key, per genome_build, per hour), bulk_job_variant_count_histogram, cache_hit_rate_per_endpoint, clinvar_significance_distribution (what fraction of queried variants have each ClinVar classification)

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster in us-central1; FastAPI deployment (3-10 pods, HPA on RPS), Celery worker deployment (2-20 pods, KEDA scaling on Redis queue depth), ClickHouse StatefulSet (3 nodes, persistent NVMe volumes), Redis (Cloud Memorystore), PostgreSQL (Cloud SQL)
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: mypy type check, pytest with ClickHouse testcontainer, Docker build and push to Artifact Registry, Helm chart deploy to GKE via gke-deploy action; staging deploy on main merge, production deploy on release tag
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping FastAPI metrics (annotation_latency_histogram, annotation_request_counter), Celery task metrics via celery-exporter, ClickHouse system.query_log metrics; Grafana dashboards for SLA compliance (p99 annotation latency per endpoint); Datadog APM for distributed trace correlation
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm rollback to previous release on failed health check; ClickHouse data version tracked in annotation_version column - schema changes require coordinated migration with semantic versioning

target_output.thinking_summary: First-pass architecture for a high-scale clinical genomics annotation API. Key design decisions: ClickHouse MergeTree with (genome_build, chrom, pos, ref, alt) primary key for sub-second variant lookups, scoped API key authentication with Redis rate limiting, HIPAA-compliant logging that excludes patient identifiers, and Celery workers with KEDA autoscaling for bulk VCF jobs. Per manifest plan_quality=flawed, I will introduce a concrete flaw: the data_model section duplicates a key name ('api_key_id' appears as both a join column and an entity definition), and the compliance section claims BAA enforcement but the API design has no mechanism to verify BAA status before accepting clinical VCF submissions - an auditor would flag this gap.
target_output.title: Genomics Variant Annotation API Service Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A high-scale partner-facing API service for genomic variant annotation, built with Python 3.12 FastAPI on GCP GKE Autopilot with ClickHouse columnar storage for 750M+ gnomAD v4 variants. Provides sub-second synchronous annotation for single variants and small batches, and asynchronous Celery-based bulk annotation for large VCF files. Scoped API key authentication with Redis rate limiting, HIPAA-compliant PHI-free logging, and OpenAPI 3.1 documentation serve clinical sequencing lab and research pipeline integrations.
target_output.architecture_overview: API-only service with no browser frontend. FastAPI application on GKE Autopilot serves REST endpoints for real-time single-variant annotation and bulk VCF job management. ClickHouse 3-node cluster holds the complete gnomAD v4 population frequency data (750M variants), ClinVar pathogenicity classifications (2M entries), and CADD precomputed scores, indexed by (genome_build, chrom, pos, ref, alt) MergeTree primary key for sub-second lookup. PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL manages API key records, job metadata, and billing. Redis on Cloud Memorystore handles per-key rate limiting counters and bulk job status. Celery workers on GKE scale via KEDA for bulk VCF annotation jobs, reading input VCF files from GCS and writing annotated results back to GCS. All annotation requests from partner clinical labs require a valid scoped API key; HIPAA audit events are logged to GCP Cloud Logging with 6-year retention.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI 0.111, pydantic v2, httpx, clickhouse-driver, asyncpg, celery[redis] 5.3, KEDA 2.x for Celery scaling. Annotation databases: ClickHouse 24.x (3-node MergeTree cluster, 9TB NVMe total), PostgreSQL 16 Cloud SQL, Redis 7 Cloud Memorystore. Reference data: gnomAD v4 (GRCh38, 750M variants), ClinVar 2025-03 release, CADD v1.7 precomputed scores. Security: Argon2 API key hashing via argon2-cffi, GCP Secret Manager via External Secrets Operator. Observability: Prometheus, Grafana, Datadog APM, Celery Flower. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, Helm 3, gke-deploy. Storage: GCS for VCF input/output with 72-hour lifecycle policy.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Real-Time Single Variant Annotation: POST /v1/variants/annotate with single variant (chrom, pos, ref, alt, genome_build); returns gnomAD population frequencies, ClinVar significance, review status, and CADD score in < 100ms p50. Small Batch Synchronous Annotation: same endpoint accepts up to 10,000 variants in request body; ClickHouse IN query returns all matched annotations within 1s p99. Bulk VCF Job Submission: POST /v1/jobs accepts GCS signed URL to input VCF file; Celery workers parse, normalize, and annotate in parallel; results written to GCS output path. Job Status and Result Delivery: GET /v1/jobs/{job_id} returns status and progress; GET /v1/jobs/{job_id}/results returns signed GCS URL to annotated VCF. API Key Management: admin-scoped endpoints for key creation, rotation, permission updates, and rate limit adjustment. Annotation Versioning: every response includes annotation_version field identifying the gnomAD and ClinVar release used.
target_output.system_components: FastAPI Annotation Service (3-10 GKE pods, HPA on annotation_request_rate). FastAPI Job Service (2-5 GKE pods, handles job submission and status). Celery Worker Fleet (2-20 GKE pods, KEDA scaling on Redis bulk-jobs queue depth). ClickHouse StatefulSet (3 replicas with ZooKeeper coordination, persistent NVMe PVs). PostgreSQL 16 Cloud SQL (HA with read replica for reporting). Redis Cloud Memorystore (rate limiting, job status, annotation result cache). GCS input-vcf bucket (72-hour lifecycle delete rule for HIPAA VCF deletion compliance). GCS results bucket (signed URL access, 7-day lifecycle for completed job results). Celery Flower (Celery worker monitoring, internal only). OpenAPI 3.1 docs endpoint (/docs, partner-accessible).
target_output.workflows: Real-Time Annotation Workflow: partner sends POST /v1/variants/annotate with X-API-Key header and request body {variants: [{chrom, pos, ref, alt, genome_build}]}; FastAPI dependency resolves key from Redis cache (Argon2 hash lookup falls back to PostgreSQL), checks annotate_realtime permission, increments Redis rate limit counter (returns 429 if exceeded); AnnotationService builds ClickHouse SELECT query on genomic_variants WHERE genome_build=? AND chrom=? AND pos=? AND ref=? AND alt=? IN (...); ClickHouse returns annotated rows in < 100ms for single variants; response includes gnomad_af, gnomad_af_popmax, clinvar_significance, cadd_phred, annotation_version; HIPAA audit event written to Cloud Logging with api_key_id and variant_count only. Bulk VCF Job Workflow: partner uploads VCF to GCS via presigned PUT URL obtained from POST /v1/jobs/upload-url; partner submits job via POST /v1/jobs with gcs_input_path; API creates annotation_jobs record with status=pending; Celery worker pulls job, streams VCF from GCS, parses CHROM/POS/REF/ALT fields, normalizes to canonical representation, submits ClickHouse batch INSERT SELECT for annotation, writes annotated VCF to GCS results path; job status updated to completed; partner polls GET /v1/jobs/{job_id} until status=completed, then retrieves signed GCS result URL.
target_output.data_model: ClickHouse genomic_variants MergeTree: genome_build LowCardinality(String), chrom LowCardinality(String), pos UInt32, ref String, alt String, gnomad_af Float64, gnomad_af_popmax Float64, gnomad_ac UInt32, gnomad_an UInt32, clinvar_significance LowCardinality(String), clinvar_review_stars UInt8, cadd_phred Float32, sift_score Float32 NULLABLE, polyphen2_score Float32 NULLABLE, annotation_version LowCardinality(String), ORDER BY (genome_build, chrom, pos, ref, alt), SETTINGS index_granularity=8192. PostgreSQL api_keys: id UUID PK, key_hash VARCHAR NOT NULL (Argon2 hash), client_name VARCHAR, permissions JSONB, rate_limit_per_minute INT, baa_signed BOOLEAN DEFAULT false, active BOOLEAN, created_at, last_used_at. PostgreSQL annotation_jobs: id UUID PK, api_key_id UUID FK REFERENCES api_keys(id), api_key_id VARCHAR [FLAW: duplicate column name 'api_key_id' - this entity definition is internally inconsistent], status VARCHAR, input_vcf_gcs_path VARCHAR, result_gcs_path VARCHAR, variant_count INT, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ NULLABLE. PostgreSQL api_usage_daily: api_key_id UUID FK, date DATE, request_count INT, variant_count BIGINT, PRIMARY KEY (api_key_id, date). Clinical genomics entities: patient_variant_reports (id UUID PK, patient_mrn_hash VARCHAR, sample_id VARCHAR, genome_build VARCHAR, ordering_physician_id UUID, clinical_indication VARCHAR, diagnosis_icd10 VARCHAR, report_status ENUM pending/signed/amended, signed_at TIMESTAMPTZ), clinical_variant_interpretations (id UUID PK, report_id FK, variant_key VARCHAR FK, acmg_classification VARCHAR, clinical_significance ENUM pathogenic/likely_pathogenic/VUS/benign, treatment_implications TEXT, reviewed_by_physician_id UUID).
target_output.api_design: POST /v1/variants/annotate: header X-API-Key; body {variants: [{chrom, pos, ref, alt, genome_build}], max 10000 items}; returns {annotations: [{...variant fields, gnomad_af, clinvar_significance, cadd_phred, annotation_version}]}; 200 OK or 400 invalid variant format or 429 rate limit exceeded or 503 ClickHouse timeout. POST /v1/jobs/upload-url: returns {presigned_put_url, gcs_path, expires_in_seconds}. POST /v1/jobs: body {gcs_input_path, genome_build, callback_url OPTIONAL}; returns {job_id, status:'pending', estimated_completion_minutes}. GET /v1/jobs/{job_id}: returns {job_id, status, progress_pct, variant_count, submitted_at, completed_at NULLABLE}. GET /v1/jobs/{job_id}/results: returns {signed_result_url, expires_at} (only when status=completed). GET /v1/health: unauthenticated liveness check returning ClickHouse and PostgreSQL connectivity status. GET /docs: OpenAPI 3.1 interactive documentation.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HIPAA compliance: annotation request audit logs written to GCP Cloud Logging contain only api_key_id (UUID, not key value), client_ip, timestamp, genome_build, variant_count, annotation_version - no patient identifiers, no variant sequences, no VCF content. VCF input files in GCS have 72-hour lifecycle delete rule enforced at storage layer. Partner BAA required before clinical use: api_keys.baa_signed field tracks BAA status; however, there is no API-layer enforcement checking baa_signed=true before accepting VCF submissions - this is a compliance gap (intentional flaw for auditor detection). mTLS: partner API connections use per-partner client certificates with CN=partner_name; revocation via certificate expiry rotation. API keys hashed with Argon2id (time_cost=3, memory_cost=65536) before storage; Redis cache stores hash for 5-minute TTL reducing PostgreSQL lookups. Rate limiting: Redis sliding window counter per api_key_id per minute; returns 429 with Retry-After header on exceed. GCP Secret Manager for ClickHouse password, Cloud SQL DSN, and Celery broker Redis URL.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot cluster in us-central1. FastAPI Annotation and Job services deployed as separate Kubernetes Deployments with HPA on custom annotation_request_rate metric from Prometheus adapter. Celery workers deployed as Deployment with KEDA ScaledObject targeting Redis bulk-jobs queue length (scale 2-20 pods). ClickHouse deployed as StatefulSet with ZooKeeper ensemble for distributed coordination; 3 replicas each with 3TB NVMe persistent volume for full gnomAD v4 dataset. Initial ClickHouse data load: gnomAD v4 VCF files ingested from GCS via clickhouse-client INSERT SELECT FROM s3() function during infrastructure setup; estimated 48-hour initial load for 750M rows. GitHub Actions CI: mypy strict type check, pytest with ClickHouse Docker testcontainer seeded with 10K test variants, Docker build, Helm lint, push to Artifact Registry; staging deploy on main merge via Helm upgrade; production deploy on release tag with manual approval gate. Secrets via External Secrets Operator pulling from GCP Secret Manager; no secrets in Helm values files.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics: annotation_latency_seconds (histogram by endpoint and genome_build, buckets 0.05-5s), annotation_request_total (counter by api_key_id, genome_build, status_code), bulk_job_variant_count (histogram), celery_task_queue_length (by queue name), clickhouse_query_duration_seconds (from ClickHouse system.query_log via prometheus_clickhouse_exporter), rate_limit_exceeded_total (by api_key_id). Grafana dashboards: SLA Compliance (p99 annotation latency per endpoint vs 1s target), Partner Usage (request rate and variant count by api_key_id), Bulk Job Throughput (active jobs, completion rate, failed job count), ClickHouse Performance (query cache hit rate, insert queue depth). Datadog APM: distributed traces correlating FastAPI request to ClickHouse query duration. PagerDuty: annotation_latency p99 > 2s for 5 minutes, error rate > 1%, ClickHouse cluster node unavailable.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated GCP monthly cost: GKE Autopilot (3-10 FastAPI pods plus 2-20 Celery pods) ~$800, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL HA ~$200, Cloud Memorystore Redis ~$100, ClickHouse StatefulSet compute (3x n2-highmem-8 equivalent) ~$1200, ClickHouse NVMe PVs (9TB) ~$900, GCS storage (VCF files, 72h lifecycle) ~$50. Total ~$3250/month. ClickHouse scales vertically; FastAPI and Celery scale horizontally. Partner volume-based pricing tiers cover infrastructure costs with margin.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): ClickHouse cluster setup and gnomAD v4 plus ClinVar initial data load, FastAPI annotation service with single variant and small batch endpoints, scoped API key authentication with Redis rate limiting, HIPAA audit logging to Cloud Logging, OpenAPI 3.1 documentation, GKE Autopilot deployment, GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline with ClickHouse testcontainer, pilot partner integration testing. Phase 2 (8 weeks): Celery bulk VCF job pipeline with KEDA autoscaling, GCS VCF upload and result delivery workflow, job status and result endpoints, CADD and SIFT score integration, annotation versioning with semantic version labels. Phase 3 (4 weeks): CADD v1.7 and PolyPhen-2 score tables loaded, Grafana SLA compliance dashboards, partner-specific usage reporting, rate limit tier configuration UI for admin.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python strict type hints with mypy strict mode in CI. Pydantic v2 models for all API request and response schemas with explicit field validation. ClickHouse queries must use parameterized query interface (no string formatting of user input). All logging must pass through PHI-redacting log filter that strips any field matching chrom/pos/ref/alt patterns from log payloads. API key hashing must use argon2-cffi with standardized parameters (never fall back to bcrypt or SHA-256). Integration tests must include ClickHouse testcontainer seeded with known variants including edge cases (multiallelic sites, indels, symbolic alleles). Zero-downtime schema changes via Helm pre-upgrade hooks.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: gnomAD v4 initial ClickHouse data load takes 48+ hours, blocking MVP timeline. Mitigation: load data in parallel batches from GCS during infrastructure provisioning week before code freeze. Risk: ClickHouse node failure during bulk annotation job corrupts partial result. Mitigation: ClickHouse ReplicatedMergeTree replication; Celery bulk jobs write results to GCS incrementally and support restart-from-checkpoint. Risk: partner BAA status check is missing from API enforcement layer (noted in data model as intentional flaw). A clinical lab could accept production VCF submissions before BAA is executed, creating HIPAA liability. Proper fix: add baa_signed check in API key authentication dependency before accepting annotation_bulk or annotate_realtime requests with clinical-flagged submissions. Tradeoff: gnomAD v4 GRCh37 liftover precomputed at data load time rather than query time - saves 50ms per query but requires 2x storage for dual-genome-build variant tables.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: GRCh37 liftover at data load time confirmed (avoids query-time liftover overhead). ClickHouse ReplicatedMergeTree over Distributed table for stronger consistency on annotation data. Celery with Redis broker chosen over GCP Pub/Sub for bulk jobs to keep infrastructure simpler; reconsider if job volume exceeds 10K concurrent jobs. mTLS per-partner client certificates chosen over IP allowlisting for revocability.

metadata.notes: Flawed: (1) data_model annotation_jobs table has duplicate 'api_key_id' column name noted as flaw; (2) security_and_compliance claims BAA required but api_design has no baa_signed enforcement gate - compliance gap explicitly called out in risks_and_tradeoffs for auditor detection.

project_goal.value: CLI tool for research computing centers to analyze HPC cluster job accounting logs and generate cost attribution reports, identifying wasteful resource reservations, idle node time, and over-allocated memory to help PIs optimize grant expenditures on compute allocations
project_goal.rationale: HPC centers report 30-40% of allocated compute hours go unused; cost attribution reports enable grant-funded PIs to right-size future allocations

target_users.value: HPC system administrators, research computing staff, and principal investigators reviewing grant expenditures
target_users.rationale: Admins run cluster-wide analysis; PIs inspect per-project summaries

project_class.rationale: Command-line tool installed on HPC login node; no web interface or hosted service

capabilities.rationale: SLURM log parsing, DuckDB analytical queries, report generation; no frontend or auth capability needed

complexity_level.rationale: SLURM accounting format parsing and DuckDB query optimization are non-trivial; multi-format report output adds scope

risk_level.rationale: Read-only analysis tool; no production system modification risk

data_sensitivity.rationale: SLURM accounting data contains job resource usage and PI/project account names; internal institutional data

external_exposure.rationale: Tool runs on HPC login node with local filesystem access to SLURM accounting exports; no network exposure

access_model.value: No authentication; OS-level file permissions control access to SLURM accounting exports; tool run under invoking user identity
access_model.rationale: CLI tool respects HPC cluster file permissions; no independent access control needed

feature_scope.value: SLURM sacct log parsing, DuckDB-powered analytical queries for efficiency metrics, per-PI and per-project cost attribution, idle node detection, over-allocated memory identification, multi-format output (JSON, CSV, HTML), optional Slack/email delivery
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for grant expenditure reporting needs

mvp_scope.value: SLURM sacct TSV parsing, efficiency metric calculation (actual vs allocated CPU and memory ratios), per-PI cost attribution report in JSON and CSV formats
mvp_scope.rationale: Core reporting capability needed for upcoming grant review cycle

security_baseline.value: Read-only filesystem access to SLURM accounting exports; no network connections from tool except optional Slack/email delivery; no credential storage
security_baseline.rationale: Minimal attack surface for local CLI tool

frontend_stack.value: N/A - CLI tool with no UI; HTML output is static report generation only
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI tool outputs to stdout and files, not a browser-based interface

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Cobra for command parsing
backend_stack.rationale: Go single binary distribution simplifies HPC cluster deployment; Cobra provides structured subcommand and flag handling

data_platform.value: DuckDB for in-process analytical SQL over SLURM accounting data files
data_platform.rationale: DuckDB executes complex aggregation queries directly on CSV/TSV files without a database server; ideal for HPC login node deployment

hosting_target.value: HPC cluster login node install via binary download or package manager
hosting_target.rationale: Single statically-linked binary for easy HPC deployment without dependency management

privacy_retention_policy.value: Tool does not store data; reads from existing SLURM accounting files; generated reports stored by user at chosen path
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No persistent storage by the tool itself

future_scope.value: PBS/Torque scheduler support, trend analysis across multiple monthly reports, web dashboard for interactive exploration
future_scope.rationale: Multi-scheduler support and trend analysis requested by HPC centers using non-SLURM schedulers

constraints.value: Must compile to a static binary with no shared library dependencies for HPC node deployment; DuckDB Go bindings are CGO and require careful static linking
constraints.rationale: HPC login nodes typically lack package managers and have restricted environments

observability_baseline.value: Structured logging to stderr with --verbose flag; --dry-run flag shows DuckDB query plan without executing
observability_baseline.rationale: CLI observability via stderr logging and query plan preview

execution_preference.value: TDD with Go test, small releases with semantic versioning, GitHub Actions for CI
execution_preference.rationale: Standard Go toolchain conventions

project.goal: Analyze SLURM job accounting logs and generate per-PI cost attribution reports
project.target_users: HPC sysadmins and PIs

data.input: SLURM sacct TSV exports with fields JobID, JobName, AllocTRES, ReqTRES, Elapsed, State, Account, User, Submit, Start, End

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with Go 1.22 matrix, golangci-lint, static binary build
devops.hosting: GitHub Releases binary download and Homebrew tap

constraints.binary: CGO_ENABLED=1 required for DuckDB but link statically; test on HPC target RHEL 8
constraints.testing: Table-driven unit tests plus integration tests against synthetic sacct fixtures

confirmed_decisions.analytics: DuckDB over SQLite for columnar scan performance on large sacct files
confirmed_decisions.cli: Cobra over flag package for subcommand structure

reasoner_reviews.product: The cost attribution report is the primary deliverable that PIs need for grant reporting. The efficiency metrics (actual vs allocated CPU and memory ratios) are the actionable insights. Idle node detection is valuable for admins but should be clearly separated from per-PI reports since it is a cluster-wide concern rather than a per-account concern.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: DuckDB reading SLURM sacct TSV files directly via read_csv_auto is the right approach; no ETL pipeline needed. The key DuckDB queries will be: GROUP BY Account, User aggregating elapsed time and allocated vs actual TRES resource fields. The TRES format in sacct (cpu=N,mem=Ng,gres/gpu=N) requires a custom parser before DuckDB ingestion - this is the most complex parsing task. Consider materializing parsed TRES into a DuckDB table for repeated query performance.
reasoner_reviews.security: CLI tool has minimal security surface. SLURM accounting files may contain job names that users set arbitrarily - CSV injection in HTML output must be handled by proper HTML escaping. No API keys or credentials needed for core functionality; Slack webhook URL for optional delivery should read from environment variable, not flag, to prevent shell history exposure.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: go-duckdb CGO static linking on RHEL 8 requires libstdc++ static linking and is documented in go-duckdb README. GitHub Actions runner should use a RHEL 8 or compatible container for the release build to ensure binary compatibility. Consider providing pre-built binaries for x86_64-linux and arm64-darwin for macOS users testing locally.

backend.service_design: Cobra root command with subcommands: analyze (parse sacct, run DuckDB queries, output report), validate (check sacct file format and TRES field parsability), schema (print expected sacct column layout). Global flags: --input-dir, --output-dir, --format (json|csv|html), --period (YYYY-MM or date range), --account-filter, --verbose, --dry-run
backend.business_modules: pkg/sacct: SLURM sacct TSV parser handling TRES field decomposition (cpu, mem, gres/gpu extraction). pkg/analytics: DuckDB session management, query templates for efficiency_metrics, cost_attribution, idle_nodes, overalloc_memory. pkg/report: multi-format report serializer (JSON via encoding/json, CSV via encoding/csv, HTML via html/template). pkg/deliver: optional Slack webhook and SMTP delivery
backend.failure_handling: Malformed TRES fields logged as warnings with row number; parsing continues with null values for that row. DuckDB query errors return descriptive error with --dry-run suggestion. Missing --input-dir returns actionable usage error with example command.

data.entities: job_records (job_id, job_name, account, user, alloc_cpus INT, req_cpus INT, alloc_mem_gb FLOAT, req_mem_gb FLOAT, alloc_gpus INT, elapsed_secs INT, state ENUM completed/failed/cancelled/timeout, submit_time, start_time, end_time), node_allocations (job_id FK, node_name, alloc_cpus, alloc_mem_gb, alloc_time_secs), project_accounts (account_name, pi_name, department, cost_rate_per_cpu_hour FLOAT), cost_attribution_reports (report_id, period_start, period_end, generated_at, format, output_path), efficiency_metrics (account, user, total_cpu_hours_alloc, total_cpu_hours_used, cpu_efficiency_pct, total_mem_gb_hours_alloc, total_mem_gb_hours_used, mem_efficiency_pct, job_count, waste_cost_estimate)
data.storage_design: DuckDB in-process database (no server); sacct TSV loaded via read_csv_auto into job_records DuckDB table; TRES fields pre-parsed by Go sacct package before DuckDB ingestion; optional account cost rates loaded from CSV sidecar file for dollar waste estimates
data.analytics_events: DuckDB queries: efficiency_by_account SELECT account, user, SUM(alloc_cpus*elapsed_secs)/3600 as alloc_cpu_hours, SUM(actual_cpus*elapsed_secs)/3600 as used_cpu_hours, 100.0*SUM(actual_cpus)/SUM(alloc_cpus) as cpu_efficiency FROM job_records GROUP BY account, user; idle_node_detection SELECT node_name, SUM(CASE WHEN alloc_cpus=0 THEN alloc_time_secs ELSE 0 END)/3600 as idle_hours FROM node_allocations GROUP BY node_name HAVING idle_hours > 24

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: test.yml runs go test ./... with -race and generates coverage; lint.yml runs golangci-lint with errcheck, staticcheck, gosec; release.yml triggers on tag, builds linux/amd64 with CGO_ENABLED=1 in ubuntu-22.04 container and darwin/arm64 cross-compile, uploads to GitHub Release
devops.deployment_topology: GitHub Releases binary download. Homebrew tap formula for macOS. RPM spec file for RHEL/CentOS HPC cluster package management.
devops.rollback_strategy: Semantic versioning; previous release binary available on GitHub Releases; no migration needed as tool is stateless

security.secrets_management: Slack webhook URL read from HPCREPORT_SLACK_WEBHOOK environment variable; SMTP password from HPCREPORT_SMTP_PASSWORD; never accepted as CLI flag to prevent shell history exposure
security.abuse_prevention: HTML report output uses html/template for automatic escaping of job names and user-supplied strings to prevent CSV/HTML injection from maliciously crafted job names

ISS-001.title: TRES field parser does not handle GPU resource variants (gres/gpu:a100)
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 TRES parser only handles gres/gpu key but SLURM clusters with multiple GPU types use gres/gpu:a100, gres/gpu:v100 etc. Current parser drops these values, producing zero GPU allocation figures in cost reports for GPU-heavy research groups.

ISS-002.title: HTML report does not escape job names, enabling HTML injection
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 used fmt.Sprintf for HTML output with raw job name strings. Fixed in this round by switching to html/template with automatic escaping.

focus_issues.title: TRES parser does not handle GPU variant keys (gres/gpu:a100)
focus_issues.detail: Parser drops multi-GPU-type TRES values, producing zero GPU allocation in cost reports for GPU clusters.

latest_recommendations: Fix TRES GPU variant parser to handle gres/gpu:TYPE colon-separated suffix
Add unit test fixtures with multi-GPU-type sacct records
Document supported TRES key formats in man page

convergence_state.reason: One unresolved medium issue; score improvement expected after fix

previous_audits.summary: Solid CLI architecture with DuckDB analytical engine and Cobra subcommand structure. ISS-002 HTML injection resolved. ISS-001 TRES GPU variant parser gap requires fix for GPU-intensive research centers.
strengths: DuckDB in-process analytical engine is the right choice for large sacct file aggregation without server overhead
Cobra subcommand structure with analyze/validate/schema is well-scoped
Go static binary approach is appropriate for HPC login node deployment constraints
concerns: TRES parser only handles gres/gpu, not gres/gpu:TYPE variants common on multi-GPU-type clusters
recommendations: Update TRES parser to split on colon and aggregate all gres/gpu:* values into total GPU count
Add sacct test fixtures with A100 and V100 TRES strings to parser unit tests
Add --gpu-types flag to filter reports by specific GPU resource types
